Colombia-Venezuela spat July 2010 — Colombia severed
relations briefly, restored). Overall normal regional
relations. UNASUR active. Normal_minor_disputes.

sources.citation: Reuters, Colombia-Venezuela relations rupture 2010

2011.assessment:
Normal_minor_disputes. Chavez cancer; sympathy from
ALBA partners. Building CELAC (Community of Latin
American and Caribbean States) as alternative to OAS.
CELAC founded December 2011 in Caracas. Chavez's moment
of peak regional soft power. Normal_minor_disputes with
US/West.

sources.citation: CELAC founding December 2011

2012.assessment:
Normal_minor_disputes. Chavez reelected; international
observers. ALBA peak membership. OAS engaged.
Venezuela leading UNASUR. Normal bilateral relations with
China, Russia (arms, oil). Normal_minor_disputes with US.

sources.citation: Freedom House 2012

2013.assessment:
Normal_minor_disputes. Chavez died; regional mourning.
Maduro election disputed but Latin American neighbors
recognized. US recognized with reservations. ALBA
solidarity. EU critical. Normal_minor_disputes.

sources.citation: Freedom House 2013

2014.assessment:
Partial_isolation_some_closures. La Salida protests; 43
killed. US, EU condemned. Panama OAS resolution debate.
Some Latin American neighbors distancing. But ALBA/UNASUR
defending Maduro. US Congress passed VDHRCS Act. Partial
isolation from Western democracies but regional support
divided.

sources.citation: Freedom House 2014

2015.assessment:
Partial_isolation_some_closures. Obama EO 13692
"extraordinary threat" — diplomatic rupture with US.
Maduro mobilized anti-US campaign in Latin America.
Opposition won Assembly December 2015 improving
Western relations somewhat. Partial_isolation_some_closures
with US/Western bloc.

sources.citation: Obama EO 13692, March 2015

2016.assessment:
Significant_isolation. Economic collapse; 3M+ in exile.
US, EU imposing targeted sanctions. OAS Secretary General
Almagro invoking Inter-American Democratic Charter against
Venezuela. TSJ blocking Assembly. Regional democracies
distancing from Maduro. MERCOSUR suspended Venezuela
(December 2016). Significant_isolation from democratic
hemisphere.

sources.citation: MERCOSUR suspension of Venezuela, December 2016
OAS Democratic Charter invocation, Almagro 2016

2017.assessment:
Significant_isolation. Lima Group formed August 2017
(12 LAC countries condemning Venezuela). OAS vote failed
to suspend (not enough votes) but growing isolation.
US financial sanctions (EO 13808). EU expanding individual
sanctions. ANC creation condemned internationally.
PDVSA default November 2017. Significant_isolation.

sources.citation: Lima Group formation, August 8, 2017
US EO 13808, financial sanctions August 2017

2018.assessment:
Significant_isolation. Sham election May 2018 rejected by
US, EU, Lima Group, OAS. Venezuela expelled from OAS (April
2019, effective). 55+ countries not recognizing Maduro's
new mandate. EU, Lima Group, OAS aligned against Maduro.
Only ALBA, Cuba, Russia, China, Turkey supporting.
Significant_isolation approaching pariah_status.

sources.citation: OAS Secretary General condemning 2018 Venezuelan election
Lima Group statement May 2018 (16 countries)

2019.assessment:
Pariah_status. January 2019: 50+ countries recognized
Guaidó as interim president. Venezuela expelled from OAS
effective April 2019. Comprehensive US sanctions. EU
sanctions expanding. China, Russia, Cuba, Bolivia (under
Morales), Nicaragua still supporting Maduro. Venezuela
operating outside Western-led international order.
Pariah_status from Western/democratic perspective.

sources.citation: OAS expulsion of Venezuela, April 2019
50+ countries recognizing Guaidó, January 2019

2020.assessment:
Pariah_status. Guaidó parallel government still recognized
by ~50 countries but declining momentum. December 2020
elections boycotted — no international legitimacy.
EU, US, Lima Group maintaining non-recognition of Maduro.
ICC preliminary investigation into Venezuela opened.
Russia/China continuing support. Pariah_status.

sources.citation: Freedom House 2020

2021.assessment:
Significant_isolation. Guaidó parallel government losing
international support (some countries withdrawing
recognition). Mexico City negotiations — Maduro engaging
diplomatically. November 2021 regional elections —
some international observers. Moving from pariah_status
toward significant_isolation as negotiations open.

sources.citation: Freedom House 2021

2022.assessment:
Significant_isolation. Guaidó parallel government dissolved
December 2022. Chevron license reopening. Maduro gaining
bilateral recognition from some countries. Still isolated
from Western democracies but trajectory improving.
Significant_isolation (less than 2019-2020 pariah level).

sources.citation: Freedom House 2022

2023.assessment:
Partial_isolation_some_closures. Barbados Accord October
2023: Maduro negotiating with US/opposition. Sanctions
relief exchanged for electoral commitments. Some normalization
of bilateral relations. Still not full integration but
meaningful diplomatic re-engagement. Partial_isolation_some_closures.

sources.citation: Barbados Agreement 2023

2024.assessment:
Significant_isolation. July 28 election fraud →
international condemnation. US, EU, OAS, Lima Group
rejected Maduro's declared victory. Sanctions reimposed.
González claimed presidency from exile. ~60 countries
recognized González as legitimate. Maduro maintained
by military and Russia/China/Cuba support. Significant_isolation.

sources.citation: International reaction to July 2024 Venezuelan election

2025.assessment:
Significant_isolation. Maduro inaugurated January 10,
2025 amid protests. US, EU, most LAC democracies
rejected inauguration. González claiming presidency.
Sanctions maintained/expanded. Russia/China/Cuba supporting
Maduro. Venezuela in significant diplomatic isolation
from Western-led international order.

sources.citation: International reaction to Maduro inauguration January 2025

1995.assessment:
FDI ~4.2% of GDP. Oil sector investment high under apertura petrolera (oil opening)
launched by Caldera government. PDVSA strategic associations with ExxonMobil,
ConocoPhillips, ChevronTexaco, Shell for Orinoco heavy oil belt development.
Pre-Chavez democratic stability and pro-investment environment. Conoco/Exxon
Cerro Negro and Hamaca heavy oil projects under negotiation.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

1996.assessment:
FDI ~4.8% of GDP. Apertura petrolera reaching full pace. Oil consortium contracts
signed. Orinoco heavy oil upgrader projects (Cerro Negro, Hamaca, Sincor, PetroCedeño)
attracting multi-billion dollar investment commitments. Banking and telecom also
receiving FDI. Currency stabilization under Agenda Venezuela improving investment
environment.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

1997.assessment:
FDI ~6.0% of GDP. Peak apertura petrolera year. All four Orinoco heavy crude
upgrader projects (Cerro Negro, Hamaca, Sincor, PetroCedeño) in active construction
phase. Massive capital inflows for upgrader infrastructure. Third oil opening round.
Political stability remaining under Caldera. Privatization of CANTV and other
state companies attracting additional FDI.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

1998.assessment:
FDI ~4.8% of GDP. Declining from 1997 peak. Oil price crash ($10/bbl 1998) dampening
new oil investment decisions. Chavez election (December 1998) creating investor
uncertainty about future of apertura. Orinoco projects continuing construction.
Pre-regime change year; political transition risk emerging.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

1999.assessment:
FDI ~3.5% of GDP. Significant decline. Chavez inauguration (February 1999) and
constituent assembly process creating investor uncertainty. New Hydrocarbons Law
discussions threatening apertura contracts. Orinoco projects continuing (contractual
obligations) but new investment commitments stalled. GDP recession (-6%) reducing
FDI as percent GDP partly because GDP fell.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2000.assessment:
FDI ~3.3% of GDP. Moderate level maintained by ongoing Orinoco projects and
oil sector operations. Oil price recovery (OPEC discipline) not translating
to new investment due to policy uncertainty. Hydrocarbons Law 2001 being
prepared (would fundamentally alter contracts). Telecommunications, retail
FDI continuing.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2001.assessment:
FDI ~2.9% of GDP. Declining. Hydrocarbons Law (November 2001) introduced
by enabling law raising royalties and limiting private participation—alarmed
oil sector investors. Land Law, banking law reforms creating uncertainty.
Political polarization increasing. Post-9/11 global FDI slowdown compounding.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2002.assessment:
FDI ~2.2% of GDP. Sharp decline. April 2002 coup attempt and reversal created
extreme political uncertainty. General strike/oil sector stoppage (December 2002-
February 2003) halted oil production. No new investment decisions. Capital flight
accelerating. Currency controls introduced February 2003. PDVSA operational
continuity at risk during strike.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2003.assessment:
FDI ~1.4% of GDP. Near trough. Post-strike economy severely disrupted. CADIVI
currency controls (Feb 2003) restricting capital flows and profit repatriation.
Investors unable to convert bolivars to dollars. GDP contracted 7.8%. Recall
referendum preparation further distracting policy. Orinoco projects operational
but no new oil commitments.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2004.assessment:
FDI ~2.5% of GDP. Recovery. Chavez won recall referendum (August 2004); political
stability returned. Oil price boom ($40/bbl+) attracting operational investment
in existing oil fields. GDP boom (+18.3%). New nationalizations not yet aggressive.
China, Russia beginning strategic investments. Petrobras (Brazil) expanding.
Despite CADIVI controls, oil sector investment continuing.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2005.assessment:
FDI ~2.4% of GDP. Moderate. New Hydrocarbons Law enforced; PDVSA renegotiating
operating agreements to give PDVSA majority control. Foreign operators migrating
to mixed enterprise model (PDVSA 60%+ minimum). Some companies accepting;
others beginning exit planning. Oil-backed FDI from China, Russia, Belarus
replacing Western multinationals.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2006.assessment:
FDI ~1.9% of GDP. Declining. Total Control of Hydrocarbons decree forcing all
remaining operators into PDVSA-controlled joint ventures. Windfall oil tax (ISLR +
extraction tax) increasing. Non-oil FDI deterred by exchange controls and price
controls. Chavez re-elected December 2006 with mandate for deeper socialism.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2007.assessment:
FDI ~0.9% of GDP. Sharp decline. Major nationalization wave begins: CANTV
(telecom) renationalized, SENECA (electricity) nationalized, Orinoco heavy
oil belt upgrader projects nationalized (ExxonMobil and ConocoPhillips refused
new terms and left; Shell, BP, Total, Statoil accepted). Significant FDI
transformation from Western multinationals to state-to-state deals with
China (CNOOC, CNPC), Russia (Rosneft, Lukoil).

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2008.assessment:
FDI ~1.2% of GDP. Slight recovery from 2007 trough driven by oil price boom
($100+/bbl) attracting state company investment from China, Russia, Iran, Belarus.
China signing oil-for-loans agreements providing some FDI-equivalent investment.
Western FDI minimal; PDVSA-dominated investment model. Continued cement, food
company nationalizations (Cemex, Holcim, Conferry).

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2009.assessment:
FDI ~0.4% of GDP. Very low. Oil price crash from 2008 peak sharply reducing
China/Russia investment commitments. Global financial crisis deterring any
remaining private FDI. Further nationalizations (Techint steel, Sidetur, Conindustria
members). PDVSA unable to operate as investment vehicle due to financial pressure.
Venezuela GDP -3.2%.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2010.assessment:
FDI ~0.3% of GDP. Near collapse. Nationalization wave continuing: banking
sector (Banco de Venezuela, Banfoandes), agricultural sector expropriations.
Property rights effectively eliminated. ExxonMobil and ConocoPhillips winning
ICSID arbitration against Venezuela deterring new investors. Profit
repatriation effectively blocked by CADIVI. GDP recovered modestly (+-1.5%).

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2011.assessment:
FDI ~1.5% of GDP. Moderate recovery. Oil price high ($100+/bbl) enabling
China fund loans ($20B+ China-Venezuela Fund) generating investment-equivalent
inflows in oil sector. China construction companies active in housing projects
(Gran Misión Vivienda). Russia arms deals creating associated investment.
Non-oil private FDI negligible.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2012.assessment:
FDI ~1.6% of GDP. Moderate, oil-dependent. Chavez re-elected (October 2012);
market certainty about continued state-led model. Chinese state company investment
continuing: CNOOC-PDVSA Sinovensa joint venture active. Huawei/ZTE telecom
infrastructure investment. Non-oil private investment essentially absent.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2013.assessment:
FDI ~1.1% of GDP. Declining. Chavez died March 2013; Maduro narrowly won
April election. Political uncertainty. GDP at nominal peak ($13,672 per capita)
but economic distortions severe (shortages, multi-tier exchange rate). PDVSA
debt accumulating. China reducing new loan commitments. Currency depreciation
beginning to erode investment values.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2014.assessment:
FDI ~0.6% of GDP. Very low. Oil price beginning to fall in H2 2014. La Salida
protests (February-May 2014) and crackdown adding political risk. GDP entering
recession (-3.9%). CADIVI/SICAD parallel exchange system dysfunctional. PDVSA
debt service increasingly straining. China loans being repaid in oil shipments
rather than generating new investment.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2015.assessment:
FDI ~0.3% of GDP. Near collapse. Oil price crash to $50/bbl eliminating oil
revenue. GDP -6.2%; inflation surpassing 100%. PDVSA production declining.
All private sector investment effectively absent. China suspending new loans.
Opposition won Assembly supermajority (December 2015) but could not reverse
economic collapse. Currency controls now multilayered and dysfunctional.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2016.assessment:
FDI approximately -0.2% of GDP. Negative net FDI outflows as remaining foreign
companies exiting. GDP -17%. Oil production declining sharply. Hyperinflation
entering. Complete shutdown of private investment. Companies repatriating
remaining equipment and capital where possible. PDVSA unable to pay suppliers;
IOC contractors withdrawing. Net outflows as disinvestment exceeds any new inflows.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2017.assessment:
FDI approximately -0.5% of GDP. Negative. Constituent assembly crisis (July
2017); US financial sector sanctions (August 2017, EO 13808) blocking US
investors from new PDVSA/government debt transactions. PDVSA default November
2017. GDP -15%. Oil production 1.9M bbl/day declining. Remaining Western
companies exiting. Russian Rosneft holding PDVSA assets as collateral for loans.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2018.assessment:
FDI approximately -0.8% of GDP. Deeply negative. Oil production fell to 1.2M
bbl/day and declining rapidly. GDP -19%. Hyperinflation (65,374%). PDVSA assets
increasingly held by Russia (Rosneft $1.5B+ stake in PdVSA assets). Most Western
companies fully exited. Gold and coltan mining attracting some informal Chinese
investment via Maduro Arc of Mines decree, not measured in formal FDI.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2019.assessment:
FDI approximately -0.4% of GDP. Negative. US comprehensive sanctions on PDVSA
(January 28, 2019) barring all US-dollar transactions. Dual-government crisis
(Guaidó recognition). Oil production collapsed to ~700K bbl/day by year-end.
Rosneft exited Venezuelan assets under US secondary sanctions threat (March 2020).
Economy so contracted that denominator (GDP) also collapsed, making negative
FDI values smaller as percent of tiny GDP.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report; World Bank WDI

2020.assessment:
FDI approximately -0.3% of GDP. Negative but stabilizing at low levels.
Economy near bottom (GDP trough ~$1,548 per capita). COVID collapse compounding
economic crisis. PDVSA production at 300-500K bbl/day. Cuba, China, Russia
providing limited operational support. Iran resuming oil services after US
sanctions. Some disinvestment continuing as remaining multinationals complete exits.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report 2021; World Bank WDI

2021.assessment:
FDI approximately 0.1% of GDP. Marginal positive. Dollarization partially
legalized (2019) stabilizing some economic activity. Small-scale FDI from
Colombia (trade investment), Spanish companies in services, China operational
investment. Oil production slowly recovering to ~600-700K bbl/day. Maduro
dollarization decree attracting informal investment. Economy stabilizing
at devastated level.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report 2022; World Bank WDI

2022.assessment:
FDI ~0.3% of GDP. Small positive. Oil production recovering to ~700-800K bbl/day.
Chevron granted limited license renewal (November 2022) after Barbados talks
beginning. Service sector investment in Caracas, Maracaibo (restaurants, retail)
under dollarization. China continuing operational oil investment. Maduro economic
opening attracting limited private sector.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report 2023; World Bank WDI

2023.assessment:
FDI ~0.4% of GDP. Marginal. Barbados Accord (October 2023) generating brief
investment optimism; OFAC General License 44 allowing some investment activity.
Chevron operating. Oil production reaching ~900K bbl/day. Turkish, Colombian,
Spanish private investment in consumer sectors. Sanctions partial relief
window attracting limited engagement. Economy growing ~5%.

sources.citation: UNCTAD World Investment Report 2024

2024.assessment:
FDI ~0.2% of GDP estimated. Declining again. US reimposed comprehensive sanctions
after July 2024 election fraud; GL44 revoked April 2024 (pre-election); Chevron
license under review. Investment optimism from 2023 reversed. Political crisis
over election results (Maduro declared winner against credible evidence of
opposition win) re-isolated Venezuela. Economy slowing.

sources.citation: UNCTAD 2024 estimates; IMF WEO

2025.assessment:
FDI ~0.2% of GDP projected. Minimal. Comprehensive sanctions continuing under
US and EU; Chevron operating under special license (renewed but contested).
China and Iran providing operational oil investment. No significant Western
FDI. Maduro authoritarian consolidation post-2024 election crisis. Oil
production stagnant at ~900K bbl/day. Fundamentally uninvestable environment
outside state-to-state arrangements.

sources.citation: UNCTAD projections 2025

No significant foreign military presence. Democratic
Venezuela with full sovereignty. US military cooperation
(counternarcotics joint operations near Colombia border)
but invited and limited. FANB fully in control.
No_foreign_presence_full_sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 1995, Venezuela

No foreign military presence. Full sovereignty. Limited
US counternarcotics cooperation. No_foreign_presence_full_sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 1996

No foreign military presence. Full sovereignty. Pre-election
period. Normal military relations. No_foreign_presence_full_sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 1997

1998.assessment:
No foreign military presence. Full sovereignty. Chavez
elected December 1998. FANB institutional. Normal defense
posture. No_foreign_presence_full_sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 1998

1999.assessment:
No foreign military presence. Full sovereignty. Chavez
inaugurated; military restructured with loyalists. Cuba
advisors beginning (medical, intelligence). Military
formally under constitutional control. No_foreign_presence_full_sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 1999

2000.assessment:
No foreign military presence. Full sovereignty. Cuba
sending doctors/intelligence advisors as part of Cuba-
Venezuela alliance. These are civilian advisors, not
military forces. US military cooperation ending. Full
sovereignty. No_foreign_presence_full_sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2000

2001.assessment:
No foreign military presence. Full sovereignty. Cuba
civil-military advisors expanding (education, health,
intelligence). US military cooperation formally terminated
by Chavez post-9/11. FANB under Chavez civilian-military
control. No_foreign_presence_full_sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2001

2002.assessment:
Limited_invited_advisors. Cuba expanding advisory presence
post-coup (April 2002) — Chavez welcomed Cuban security
intelligence advisors to help prevent future coup. G2
(Cuban intelligence) reportedly helping SEBIN. Invited
but growing presence. Limited_invited_advisors.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2002

2003.assessment:
Limited_invited_advisors. Cuban civil-military presence
expanding (Mision Barrio Adentro — 10,000+ Cuban doctors).
Cuban intelligence advisors growing. Russia defense
purchases (AK-47s, helicopters). Limited_invited_advisors
from Cuba/Russia.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2003

2004.assessment:
Limited_invited_advisors. Cuba providing estimated
20,000-40,000 personnel (doctors, teachers, advisors).
Cuba-Venezuela strategic alliance deepening. Russia
beginning major arms sales (2005: 100,000 AK-103s;
helicopters). Limited_invited_advisors — invited
bilateral arrangement not occupation.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2004

2005.assessment:
Limited_invited_advisors. Cuba massive presence (~50,000
personnel including doctors, intelligence, education).
Russia: $3.4B arms deal (2006); Su-30 fighters, T-72
tanks, helicopters. These are invited bilateral programs
not foreign military presence in sovereignty-threatening
sense. Limited_invited_advisors.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2005

2006.assessment:
Limited_invited_advisors. Cuba massive advisory presence.
Russia delivering arms (Su-30 fighters). Iran military
cooperation agreements. Belarus defense cooperation.
Chavez diversifying military partnerships away from US.
All invited. Limited_invited_advisors.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2006

2007.assessment:
Limited_invited_advisors. Cuba intelligence advisors
integrated into SEBIN structure. Russia continuing arms
deliveries. FARC connections emerging (laptops seized
in Colombia 2008 showing Chavez-FARC links). Limited_invited_advisors
from Cuba/Russia; FARC presence not invited advisors
but separate.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2007

2008.assessment:
Limited_invited_advisors. Cuba massive civilian-military
advisory presence. Russia: $10B arms credit agreed.
Chavez expelled US ambassador; US military cooperation
fully terminated. Cuba/Russia primary military partners.
Limited_invited_advisors.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2008

2009.assessment:
Limited_invited_advisors. Russia joint military exercises
(Black Sea fleet visit to La Guaira 2008; continuing).
Cuba G2 advisors in intelligence. Russia arms deliveries
continuing. Limited_invited_advisors.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2009

2010.assessment:
Limited_invited_advisors. Cuba advisory presence. Russia
military cooperation (loans for arms). Chavez accusations
from Colombia about FARC in Venezuelan territory.
FARC presence in border states not invited by Chavez
officially but tolerated. Limited_invited_advisors.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2010

2011.assessment:
Limited_invited_advisors. Cuba continuing massive advisory
presence (Chavez cancer treatment in Cuba — also deepening
dependency). Russia arms deliveries. FARC in border
states expanding. Limited_invited_advisors (Cuba/Russia);
FARC presence becoming harder to categorize as invited.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2011

2012.assessment:
Limited_invited_advisors. Cuba continuing. Russia arms.
Chavez receiving cancer treatment in Cuba. Iranian
military-civil cooperation (joint factory, drones).
FARC presence in Apure growing. All limited and invited
or tolerated. Limited_invited_advisors.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2012

2013.assessment:
Limited_invited_advisors. Chavez died in Cuba (March 2013).
Cuba deepening involvement in Venezuelan political/security
structure under Maduro. Russia maintaining arms partnership.
FARC in border states. Cuba's influence over Venezuelan
intelligence/security expanding. Limited_invited_advisors
though Cuba's role growing beyond typical advisory.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2013

2014.assessment:
Significant_invited_forces. Cuba's role in Venezuelan
security becoming more extensive: G2 training SEBIN,
FANB intelligence, advising on protest suppression (La
Salida). Russia: continuing arms deliveries. FARC in
border states growing. Cuba's military-intelligence
presence now significant_invited_forces.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2014
HRW reports on Cuban intelligence advising Venezuelan security forces

2015.assessment:
Significant_invited_forces. Cuba: estimated 15,000-20,000
civilian-military personnel; intelligence deeply integrated
into Venezuelan security apparatus. Russia: maintaining
arms partnership. FARC border presence growing.
Obama EO 13692 sanctions partially targeting Cuban-
linked officials. Significant_invited_forces.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2015

2016.assessment:
Significant_invited_forces. Cuba security presence
extensive. Russia: S-300 missiles deployed. Economic
collapse driving FARC/ELN expansion into Venezuelan
territory. Colectivos as parallel security forces.
Cuba G2 integral to Maduro government survival.
Significant_invited_forces.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2016

2017.assessment:
Significant_invited_forces. Cuba intelligence advisors
integral to SEBIN operations and protest suppression.
Russia military-financial support (arms, debt relief).
FARC (Colombia peace deal) releasing forces into
Venezuelan territory. Drone assassination attempt August
2017 (actually 2018) suggesting deep internal military
tensions. Significant_invited_forces.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2017

2018.assessment:
Significant_invited_forces. Cuba G2 deeply embedded in
Venezuelan security. Drone assassination attempt August
2018 confirmed deep military factional tensions. Russia
military agreements. FARC/ELN now occupying significant
border territory. Iran military-technical cooperation.
Significant_invited_forces.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2018

2019.assessment:
Significant_invited_forces. January 2019: Russia deployed
~100 military advisors and two Tu-160 bombers (December 2018).
Cuba G2 integral to Maduro survival (April 2019 uprising
suppressed partly via Cuban intelligence). FARC/ELN
in border states. Russian financial/military support
critical to Maduro surviving Guaidó challenge.
Significant_invited_forces.

sources.citation: Reuters, Russian military advisors in Venezuela 2019
IISS Military Balance 2019

2020.assessment:
Significant_invited_forces. Russia military advisors
continuing. Cuba G2 embedded in SEBIN. May 2020: Operation
Gedeon mercenary incursion from Colombia — quickly
suppressed; demonstrated security apparatus strength.
FARC/ELN in Apure/Zulia/Amazonas. Significant_invited_forces.

2021.assessment:
Significant_invited_forces. Russia continuing advisory
presence. Cuba G2 deeply embedded. March-April 2021
Operation Escudo Bolivariano in Apure (vs. dissident FARC):
FANB mobilized against armed groups in own territory —
contested_mixed_presence in border areas. Overall
significant_invited_forces (Cuba/Russia invited) plus
contested border areas.

2022.assessment:
Significant_invited_forces. Russia continuing advisory
presence (despite Ukraine war). Cuba G2 embedded.
Guaidó dissolved December 2022. FARC/ELN continuing
territorial presence. Russia distracted by Ukraine but
maintaining Venezuela relationship as geopolitical
signaling. Significant_invited_forces.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2022

2023.assessment:
Significant_invited_forces. Cuba G2 advisory remaining
integral. Russia maintaining strategic relationship.
Barbados Accord diplomatic opening but not affecting
foreign military presence. FARC/ELN continuing in border
states. Tren de Aragua territorial. Significant_invited_forces.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2023

2024.assessment:
Significant_invited_forces. Cuba G2 central to Maduro
post-election survival (assisting crackdown on protesters).
Russia maintaining advisory presence. FARC/ELN border
areas. Post-election: Maduro more dependent on Cuba/Russia
for military-security support. Significant_invited_forces.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2024

2025.assessment:
Significant_invited_forces. Maduro inaugurated January
2025. Cuba G2 and Russian advisors central to regime
survival. FARC/ELN in border states continuing.
US sanctions reimposed reducing oil revenue but
Cuba/Russia partnership maintained. Significant_invited_forces
— invited by Maduro to maintain power.

1995.assessment:
Negligible displacement. Venezuela stable democratic state under Caldera.
Net immigration country—attracting migrants from Colombia, Peru, Ecuador.
Tiny outflows consist of economic migrants, not displacement. Population
~22M; emigration minimal. No refugee flows in any meaningful sense.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 1995; IOM Venezuela

1996.assessment:
Negligible displacement. Economic difficulties (inflation, currency crisis)
generating some emigration by middle-class Venezuelans but not displacement-scale
flows. Still a net immigration destination for Colombian conflict refugees.
UNHCR presence focused on Colombian refugee intake, not Venezuelan outflow.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 1996

1997.assessment:
Negligible displacement. Venezuela stable; apertura petrolera investment boom
supporting economy. Any emigration primarily lifestyle choice of affluent
Venezuelans, not forced displacement. Net immigration from Colombia, Peru,
other Andean nations.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 1997; IOM

1998.assessment:
Negligible to minimal displacement. Chavez election (December 1998) generating
small wave of emigration by business class, political elites fearing regime
change. Oil price crash ($10/bbl) economic hardship. Total emigrants in low
tens of thousands, not displacement-scale. Still net immigration country overall.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 1998; IOM

1999.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Chavez takes office (February 1999); constituent assembly
process unsettling elites and professionals. Small-scale emigration of middle/upper
class to Spain, USA, Colombia. Cumulative emigration since 1998 election reaching
~50-100K but not displacement-level. GDP recession (-6%) adding push factor.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 1999; IOM

2000.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Continued small emigration of professionals and business
class. Chavez governing but country not yet crisis-level economically. Oil prices
recovering with OPEC discipline. Professional emigration (medical, engineering,
business) to Spain, USA, Colombia ongoing but low total numbers.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2000; IOM

2001.assessment:
Minimal displacement. Hydrocarbons Law (November 2001) and social mobilization
decrees alarming business community; political polarization increasing professional
emigration pace. Cumulative diaspora reaching ~200-300K. Still lifestyle/economic
emigration rather than forced displacement. Spain principal destination.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2001; IOM

2002.assessment:
Low-moderate displacement. April 2002 coup attempt/reversal and December
2002 general strike generating significant political-economic push factors.
Middle-class emigration accelerating. Cumulative diaspora reaching ~400-500K.
Professionals (doctors, engineers, business managers) departing in significant
numbers. Spain, USA, Colombia, Panama principal destinations.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2002; IOM Venezuela

2003.assessment:
Low-moderate displacement. Post-strike economic devastation (GDP -7.8%);
CADIVI currency controls trapping middle-class savings. Recall referendum
period. Continued professional emigration; PDVSA workforce exodus after
~18,000 oil workers fired. Cumulative diaspora ~600-700K. Spain major
recipient; Colombian border crossing also for poorer Venezuelans.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2003; IOM

2004.assessment:
Moderate-low displacement. Chavez recall referendum victory (August 2004);
political stability returned; oil boom beginning. Economic emigration moderating
slightly as economy improved. Cumulative diaspora ~700K. Some return of exiles
after stabilization. Professional emigration continuing but at slower pace.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2004; IOM

2005.assessment:
Moderate-low. Ongoing professional emigration despite oil boom; political
uncertainty about PDVSA-operating agreement renegotiation. GDP growing strongly
but business environment increasingly state-controlled. Emigration by
business owners and professionals continuing. Cumulative diaspora approaching 1M.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2005; IOM

2006.assessment:
Low-moderate displacement. Chavez re-elected December 2006; policy direction
toward socialism intensifying. Business community emigration accelerating;
medical professionals sought by Spain/USA. Nationalizations pipeline unsettling
private sector. Cumulative diaspora ~1.2M by year-end.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2006; IOM

2007.assessment:
Low-moderate displacement. CANTV nationalization, Orinoco belt nationalization
(ExxonMobil/ConocoPhillips exit). Business and professional emigration accelerating.
Socialism of 21st Century constitutional referendum (December 2007, narrowly
defeated). Cumulative diaspora ~1.5M. Spain receiving large Venezuelan wave.
USA Venezuelan immigration increasing.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2007; IOM

2008.assessment:
Low-moderate. Oil boom peak masking fundamental economic problems; some
emigration moderating as oil revenues funded social programs. Global financial
crisis (H2 2008) reducing receiving-country attractiveness. Cumulative diaspora
~1.7M. Professional emigration steady but not accelerating.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2008; IOM

2009.assessment:
Low-moderate displacement. Oil price crash (-50%); GDP -3.2%; security
deterioration (Caracas homicide rate among world's highest). Business
emigration up. Physical insecurity increasingly driving emigration in addition
to economic/political factors. Cumulative diaspora ~2M.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2009; IOM

2010.assessment:
Low-moderate. Continued emigration driven by crime, economic insecurity,
political repression. Venezuela had world's highest homicide rates. Media
crackdowns; RCTV closure (2007). Cumulative diaspora ~2.3M. Spain remained
major destination; US immigration growing. Educational emigration strong
(young professionals fleeing).

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2010; IOM

2011.assessment:
Low-moderate displacement. Continued gradual emigration. Chavez cancer diagnosis
(2011) creating political uncertainty. Economic distortions (price controls,
shortages) affecting quality of life. Security still poor. Cumulative diaspora
reaching ~2.6M. Professional brain drain accelerating.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2011; IOM

2012.assessment:
Low-moderate. Chavez re-elected (October 2012); limited political change expected.
Shortages of basic goods spreading; queues for food and medicine. Emigration
steady. Cumulative diaspora ~2.9M. Final year of relative economic normalcy
before accelerating crisis. Young Venezuelans increasingly leaving.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2012; IOM

2013.assessment:
Increasing displacement. Chavez died March 2013; Maduro narrowly won April
election amid fraud allegations. Economic mismanagement intensifying: 56%
inflation, growing shortages. Security deterioration continuing. Emigration
pace accelerating. Cumulative diaspora ~3.3M. GDP at nominal peak but
structural crisis deepening.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2013; IOM

2014.assessment:
Significant and accelerating displacement. La Salida protests (February-May
2014) with brutal crackdown (43 killed, hundreds imprisoned) generating
political refugee flows for first time. GDP entering recession (-3.9%); oil
prices beginning to crash. Food/medicine shortages spreading. UNHCR beginning
to track Venezuelan asylum seekers more carefully. Cumulative diaspora ~3.9M;
new outflows ~190K-200K/year.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2014; R4V Venezuela platform

2015.assessment:
Mass displacement beginning. GDP -6.2%; oil crash; hyperinflation entering
(121%+). Food/medicine shortages severe. Porous Colombian border seeing tens
of thousands crossing monthly. Opposition won Assembly supermajority but Maduro
refused to accept results. UNHCR formal tracking of Venezuela displacement
established. Estimated ~300-340K new departures. Cumulative diaspora ~4.2M.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2015; R4V Venezuela

2016.assessment:
Mass displacement. GDP -17%; hyperinflation (180%+); food/medicine critical
shortage. Venezuelans crossing into Colombia in hundreds of thousands. Caminantes
(walkers) beginning to travel on foot to Ecuador, Peru, Argentina. UNHCR
Latin America offices overwhelmed. ~500K new departures in 2016. Colombian
border town Cucuta receiving tens of thousands monthly. Cumulative diaspora ~4.7M.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2016; R4V Venezuela

2017.assessment:
Mass displacement accelerating rapidly. Constituent assembly crisis (July 2017);
violent crackdown on protests (125+ killed). GDP -15%; hyperinflation (2,616%).
PDVSA default (November 2017). ~800K new departures in 2017. UNHCR declared
Venezuelan displacement among world's largest crises. Cumulative to ~5.5M.
Colombia, Peru, Ecuador overwhelmed. Venezuela recognized as refugee situation
by UNHCR and R4V platform established.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2017; R4V Venezuela

2018.assessment:
Catastrophic mass displacement. GDP -19%; hyperinflation (65,374%). FAES
killings (5,287 documented). Maduro fraudulent re-election (May 2018). ~1.3-1.6M
new departures in 2018. Largest displacement crisis in Latin American history.
World's 4th largest displacement crisis globally (after Syria, Afghanistan, South Sudan).
Colombia hosting ~1.1M; Peru ~700K; Ecuador ~250K; Argentina, Chile receiving
large flows. Cumulative ~7M+.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2018; R4V Venezuela

2019.assessment:
Catastrophic displacement at peak rate. Dual government crisis (Guaidó
recognition January 2019); PDVSA sanctions; blackout crisis (March 2019).
Hyperinflation 344,510%. Oil production collapsed to ~700K bbl/day. ~1.7M
new departures in 2019. Cumulative outside Venezuela reached 4.5M by end-2019
(UNHCR count), total diaspora ~7.5M. Colombia: 1.7M; Peru: 860K; Ecuador: 330K.
UNHCR Refugee Coordinator appointed specifically for Venezuela.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2019; R4V Situation Venezuela

2020.assessment:
Mass displacement continuing at high rate but moderated by COVID border closures.
GDP trough (~$1,548/capita). COVID pandemic closed borders and slowed physical
movement; many Venezuelans trapped in transit countries. Some returned temporarily
from Colombia (200K+ estimated returns during pandemic). Net flows still massive.
Cumulative outside Venezuela ~5.4M (UNHCR). New outflows ~700K-900K despite restrictions.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2020; R4V Situation Venezuela

2021.assessment:
Mass displacement. COVID restrictions lifting; displacement resumed high pace.
Economy stabilizing (dollarization) but damage too severe for return.
Cumulative outside Venezuela ~6M (UNHCR June 2021). Colombia: 1.8M; Peru: 1.2M;
Ecuador: 470K; Chile: 450K; US: 350K+. ~1.1M new departures in 2021.
Largest displacement crisis in Western Hemisphere history.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2021; R4V Situation Venezuela

2022.assessment:
Catastrophic displacement. Cumulative reached 7.1M outside Venezuela (R4V
November 2022). Colombia: 2.5M; Peru: 1.5M; Ecuador: 530K; USA: 500K+;
Chile: 560K; Argentina: 170K. ~1.3M new departures in 2022. Continuing
despite modest economic recovery. Underlying conditions (repression, poverty,
lack of services) maintaining exodus. Largest ongoing displacement crisis globally
excluding Ukraine/Syria/Afghanistan.

sources.citation: UNHCR Global Trends 2022; R4V Situation Venezuela

2023.assessment:
Mass displacement continuing. R4V total: 7.7M+ by end 2023. Barbados Accord
(October 2023) generating brief hope but not stopping flows. ~900K-1M new
departures in 2023 (slightly lower pace than 2022 peak). Darien Gap route
saw 400K+ crossings (including Venezuelans) in 2023. USA experiencing unprecedented
Venezuelan arrivals through Mexico border. Cumulative total estimated 7.7M
(UNHCR/IOM/R4V joint estimate).

sources.citation: UNHCR/R4V Venezuela Situation 2023

2024.assessment:
Mass displacement continuing and potentially re-accelerating after election crisis.
July 2024 election: Maduro declared winner over Edmundo González despite credible
evidence of opposition win. Post-election crackdown; hundreds arrested. International
community condemned results. New political persecution wave. Additional ~800K-1M
projected departures in 2024. Cumulative ~8M+ by year-end. Darien Gap crossings
continuing at record pace. US reception policies fluctuating.

sources.citation: UNHCR/R4V Venezuela Situation 2024 reports

2025.assessment:
Mass displacement projected to continue. Post-election crackdown consolidating
Maduro rule; conditions for voluntary return absent. Sanctions maintained;
economy stagnant. US policy changes under new administration may redirect
Venezuelan flows back to South America. Cumulative projected ~8.5M+ total.
Venezuela remains largest displacement crisis in Western Hemisphere and among
the largest globally. No prospect of return without fundamental political change.

sources.citation: UNHCR/R4V projections 2025

1995.assessment:
No international sanctions against Venezuela. Democratic
government under Caldera. Normal bilateral relations with
US (counternarcotics cooperation, oil trade). Full
international standing. No_sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela Country Report 1995

1996.assessment:
No sanctions. Democratic Venezuela. Normal international
relations. US-Venezuela counternarcotics cooperation active.
Oil trade normal. No_sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela Country Report 1996

1997.assessment:
No sanctions. Pre-election period. Democratic Venezuela
with normal international standing. No_sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela 1997

1998.assessment:
No sanctions. Chavez won December 1998 in free/fair election.
US-Venezuela relations normal despite some concern about
Chavez's anti-US rhetoric. No sanctions imposed.
No_sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela 1998-1999

1999.assessment:
No sanctions. Chavez inaugurated; constituent assembly;
new constitution. US-Venezuela relations strained but
no sanctions. Oil trade continued (US largest buyer).
No_sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela 1999

2000.assessment:
No sanctions. Mega-elections; Chavez government legitimized.
US-Venezuela oil trade primary. Relations tense but
cooperative on oil. No_sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela 2000

2001.assessment:
No sanctions. 49 enabling law decrees concerning to US
but no sanctions. Post-9/11: Venezuela did not join US-led
coalition; Chavez criticized US response. Relations
deteriorating but no sanctions imposed. No_sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela 2001

2002.assessment:
No sanctions. April 2002 coup: US tacitly recognized
Carmona government (briefly); then reversed when Chavez
restored. US-Venezuela relations badly damaged. Oil
trade continued. US did not impose sanctions despite
democratic deterioration. No_sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela 2002

2003.assessment:
No sanctions. Post-coup relations damaged but oil trade
priority. OAS facilitated recall referendum process.
US monitoring Venezuelan democracy without sanctions.
Carter Center engaged. No_sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela 2003

2004.assessment:
No sanctions. Recall referendum conducted; Carter Center
certified. US critical but no sanctions. Venezuela
decertified from US counternarcotics cooperation (Majors
list) — but this is not an economic sanction.
No_sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela 2004

2005.assessment:
No sanctions. US arms sales embargo began (2006 formally).
Chavez closing US military cooperation. CONATEL law.
RCTV under pressure. But no economic sanctions yet.
US-Venezuela oil trade continues (~1.5M bbl/day to US).
No_sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela 2005

2006.assessment:
No economic sanctions. US arms sales embargo formalized.
Chavez declaring Cuba/Russia/China as preferred partners.
US-Venezuela oil trade continued despite political
hostility. No OFAC sanctions yet. No_sanctions for
economic/financial purposes.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela 2006

2007.assessment:
No economic sanctions. RCTV shut down. Chavez nationalizing
US companies (ExxonMobil, ConocoPhillips in 2007-2008).
Compensation disputes beginning. But no OFAC sanctions
imposed. US critical but prioritizing oil trade.
No_sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela 2007

2008.assessment:
No economic sanctions. Oil at $147/bbl; US still importing
~1.5M bbl/day from Venezuela. ExxonMobil/ConocoPhillips
compensation disputes via international arbitration.
Chavez expelled US ambassador (September 2008). US
expelled Venezuelan ambassador reciprocally. But no
OFAC financial sanctions. No_sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela 2008

2009.assessment:
No economic sanctions. US-Venezuela relations normalized
slightly under Obama (handshake at FTAA summit April
2009). Ambassadors returned. Obama engagement policy.
Oil trade continuing. No OFAC sanctions. No_sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela 2009

2010.assessment:
No economic sanctions. Relations tense (FARC accusations;
Colombia-Venezuela spat). Judge Afiuni jailed. Internet
censorship. But no OFAC economic sanctions on Venezuela
as a state. No_sanctions.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Venezuela 2010

2011.assessment:
No economic sanctions on Venezuela state. Some individuals
sanctioned under Kingpin Act (drug trafficking). Chavez
cancer. Iran-Venezuela military cooperation raising US
concerns. But no broad economic sanctions. No_sanctions
(state level). Targeted_individuals_only (drug traffickers).

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC, Kingpin Act designations 2011

2012.assessment:
Targeted_individuals_only. OFAC sanctioning individual
Venezuelan officials under Kingpin Act (drug trafficking).
No state-level sanctions. Chavez reelected. US-Venezuela
oil trade continuing. Relations hostile but economic
relations maintained.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC 2012 individual designations

2013.assessment:
Targeted_individuals_only. Chavez died; Maduro took over.
Increasing OFAC individual designations of Venezuelan
officials. No state sanctions. Maduro election disputed
(1.5%). US not recognizing but no sanctions. OFAC
continuing drug trafficking designations. Targeted_individuals_only.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC 2013 Venezuela designations

2014.assessment:
Targeted_individuals_only. La Salida protests; 43 killed.
Lopez jailed. US condemned but initial response limited.
December 2014: Congress passed Venezuela Defense of Human
Rights and Civil Society Act (VDHRCS) requiring sanctions
on human rights violators. Signed by Obama December 2014.
Still targeted_individuals not state-level. Growing
toward broader sanctions.

sources.citation: Venezuela Defense of Human Rights Act, December 2014

2015.assessment:
Targeted_individuals_only. March 2015: Obama Executive
Order 13692 declared Venezuela "extraordinary threat to
US national security" — sanctioned 7 specific Venezuelan
officials. Maduro responded with extreme anger. But
these remained targeted individual sanctions, not
comprehensive state sanctions. Targeted_individuals_only.

sources.citation: Obama Executive Order 13692, March 9, 2015

2016.assessment:
Targeted_individuals_only. EO 13692 expanded; more
Venezuelan officials (SEBIN, military, TSJ judges)
designated. No financial sector sanctions yet. US still
importing oil from Venezuela (~800K bbl/day). Targeted
individual sanctions expanding but not yet sectoral
or comprehensive. Targeted_individuals_only.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC, Venezuela program 2016

2017.assessment:
Sectoral_sanctions. August 2017: Trump Executive Order
13808 imposed financial sector sanctions — prohibited
US persons from dealing in new Venezuelan government
and PDVSA debt (bonds). This prevented Venezuela from
refinancing debt in US markets. Major escalation from
targeted individuals to sectoral financial sanctions.
PDVSA defaulted November 2017 partly due to this.

sources.citation: Trump Executive Order 13808, August 25, 2017
US Treasury OFAC, Venezuela financial sanctions 2017

2018.assessment:
Sectoral_sanctions. March 2018: Trump EO 13827 prohibited
Venezuelan cryptocurrency transactions (Petro). November
2018: EO 13835 expanded restrictions. Sectoral financial
sanctions expanding. US still importing some Venezuelan
oil but under restrictions. Sham election May 2018 not
triggering immediate comprehensive sanctions.
Sectoral_sanctions.

sources.citation: Trump EO 13827 (March 2018) and EO 13835 (May 2018)

2019.assessment:
Comprehensive_sanctions. January 28, 2019: Trump EO
13884 imposed comprehensive blocking sanctions on PDVSA
— all PDVSA US assets blocked, US persons prohibited
from transactions. US Citgo (PDVSA subsidiary) assets
frozen. Venezuela oil exports to US effectively ended.
Most comprehensive oil sanctions imposed. Additional
executive orders blocking all Venezuelan government
property. Comprehensive_sanctions.

sources.citation: Trump Executive Order on PDVSA, January 28, 2019
US Treasury OFAC PDVSA designation 2019

2020.assessment:
Comprehensive_sanctions. Additional EOs expanding sanctions.
August 2019: EO blocking all Venezuelan government property
in US (most comprehensive to date). EU also imposing
targeted individual sanctions. 5M+ refugees partly
driven by economic collapse from sanctions + structural
causes. Comprehensive_sanctions maintained throughout.

sources.citation: Trump EO 13884, August 2019; Comprehensive Venezuela sanctions

2021.assessment:
Comprehensive_sanctions. Biden maintained comprehensive
PDVSA/government sanctions. Chevron license (limited)
maintained. EU expanded targeted individual sanctions.
Mexico City negotiations signaling possible sanctions
relief pathway but no actual easing. Comprehensive_sanctions
continuing.

sources.citation: Biden Administration Venezuela sanctions continuation 2021

2022.assessment:
Comprehensive_sanctions with partial relief. Russia-Ukraine
war creating US interest in Venezuelan oil. November
2022: Chevron license expanded (to resume Venezuela oil
sales). December 2022: broader sanctions relief allowing
some Chevron operations. Comprehensive_sanctions partially
eased for Chevron specifically but PDVSA/government
sanctions maintaining overall. Broad_economic_humanitarian_exceptions
boundary. Comprehensive_sanctions still dominant.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Chevron License, November 2022

2023.assessment:
Broad_economic_humanitarian_exceptions. Barbados Accord
(October 2023): US agreed to six-month sanctions relief
(October 2023) on oil/gold sectors in exchange for
electoral guarantees. Significant easing allowing more
Western oil company operations. Not comprehensive easing
but meaningful partial relief. Broad_economic_humanitarian
exceptions level.

Comprehensive_sanctions reimposed. January 2024: Maduro
barred Machado; US reimposed oil/gas sanctions (April 2024).
Post-July 28 election fraud: further sanctions expansion.
US returned to near-comprehensive sanctions regime.
Chevron license maintained but other oil operations
restricted. Comprehensive_sanctions effectively reimposed.

2025.assessment:
Comprehensive_sanctions. Maduro inaugurated January 10,
2025. US maintained/expanded comprehensive sanctions.
Chevron license reviewed. EU expanded individual
sanctions list. International pressure but Maduro
retaining power through military loyalty. Venezuela

Comprehensive_sanctions.

1995.assessment:
FH CL=3. Second Caldera presidency (1994-1999). Venezuela
functioning democracy with genuine civil liberties though
weakened by economic crisis (banking crisis 1994). Press
relatively free; assembly rights observed; judicial system
weak but functional. Caracazo aftermath (1989) and 1992 coup
attempts (Chavez) had stressed institutions but democracy
survived. Partial_free status under Caldera's reform period.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1995

1996.assessment:
FH CL=3. Caldera government implementing Agenda Venezuela
economic stabilization. Civil liberties maintained despite
economic hardship. Press freedom functional; political
opposition active; judicial independence limited but present.
Chavez released from prison (1994) and building political
movement but within democratic framework.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1996

1997.assessment:
FH CL=3. Pre-election year. Chavez's MVR party organizing for
1998 elections. Traditional parties (AD/COPEI) discredited.
Civil liberties maintained; free press; right to organize.
Oil boom improving economy slightly. Peaceful political
competition ongoing within democratic framework.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1997

1998.assessment:
FH CL=3. Election year. Chavez won presidential election
December 6, 1998 with 56% of vote — free and fair election.
Traditional parties collapsed (AD/COPEI). Civil liberties
maintained through electoral process. Free press, assembly
rights, competitive elections. Democratic regime change
through legitimate vote.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1998

1999.assessment:
FH CL=4. Regime change year. Chavez inaugurated February 2,
1999. Immediately called constituent assembly referendum
(April) to replace 1961 constitution; approved by 92% vote.
New constitution (December 1999) with expanded rights but
also concentrated power. Judicial system reconstituted.
Congress dissolved. Civil liberties formally strong in new
constitution but institutional framework being reshaped.
Transition from CL=3 to CL=4 reflecting uncertainty.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1999-2000
Human Rights Watch, World Report 2000 - Venezuela

2000.assessment:
FH CL=4. Chavez won mega-elections July 2000 under new
constitution (elected to new 6-year term). Media pluralism
still present but government pressure on private media
increasing (especially RCTV, Globovision). Military loyalists
placed in key positions. Civil society organizations active.
Habilitating law (December 2000) granted Chavez decree
powers for one year — significant_restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2000-2001

2001.assessment:
FH CL=4. Chavez issuing 49 decrees under habilitating law
(November 2001) including land reform, hydrocarbon law,
fishing law — triggering major business/union opposition.
General strike called December 2001. Media still pluralist
but heavily politicized. Judiciary increasingly packed with
Chavistas. Civil liberties technically protected but
increasingly selectively enforced.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2001-2002

2002.assessment:
FH CL=5. April 2002 coup briefly removed Chavez (April 11-13);
restored after popular/military backlash. December 2002-
February 2003: general strike/oil industry shutdown. PDVSA
management fired (~18,000 workers). Media largely aligned
with opposition; government using state media aggressively.
Political polarization extreme. Severe_restrictions reflecting
systematic violation of civic rights during crisis periods.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2002-2003
Human Rights Watch, World Report 2003 - Venezuela (coup/strike)

2003.assessment:
FH CL=5. Post-oil strike aftermath; PDVSA restructured under
Chavez loyalists. Tascon List: government published names of
recall referendum signers, used to discriminate in employment.
Misiones social programs begun (Barrio Adentro, Robinson).
Judicial appointments accelerated packing courts with Chavistas.
Political discrimination against opposition becoming systematic.
Severe_restrictions continuing.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2003-2004
Human Rights Watch, Venezuela: A Decade Under Chavez, 2008 (Tascon List)

2004.assessment:
FH CL=5. Recall referendum August 2004 — Chavez won 59%.
Opposition disputed results; Carter Center certified. Social
programs (misiones) expanded with oil windfall. Law of Social
Responsibility in Radio and TV (CONATEL law) enacted —
restrictions on broadcast media content. Supreme Court expanded
from 20 to 32 justices (court-packing). Severe_restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2004-2005

2005.assessment:
FH CL=5. Congressional elections December 2005 — opposition
boycotted after electoral council changes; Chavistas won all
167 National Assembly seats. No political opposition in
legislature. Media landscape narrowing: private channels
under regulatory pressure. Criminal defamation laws used
against journalists. Oil boom (WTI ~$55/bbl) financing
social expansion. Severe_restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005-2006

2006.assessment:
FH CL=5. Chavez reelected December 2006 (63% of vote) in
competitive but unequal election. Declared "21st Century
Socialism." Announced non-renewal of RCTV license (major
critical TV channel, expired 2007). State media dominance
increasing. Civil society organizations receiving foreign
funding threatened with closure. Severe_restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006-2007

2007.assessment:
FH CL=5. May 2007: RCTV shut down — major private broadcaster
replaced by state channel TVES. First significant rollback of
press freedom. December 2007: constitutional referendum to
allow indefinite reelection and expand presidential powers
FAILED (51-49%). Chavez accepted narrow defeat — brief
democratic moment. Media pluralism severely reduced.
Severe_restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007-2008
Human Rights Watch, A Decade Under Chavez, 2008

2008.assessment:
FH CL=5. Oil at $147/bbl peak (July 2008); misiones at peak
funding. State nationalizations expanding (Cemex, banks, steel).
Regional elections November 2008 — opposition won several
governorships (Miranda, Zulia, Carabobo). Democratic competition
at subnational level still meaningful. Severe_restrictions
at national level but subnational pluralism remaining.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008-2009

2009.assessment:
FH CL=5. February 2009: constitutional amendment referendum
approved indefinite reelection (54%). Chavez moved against
opposition governors (stripped Caracas mayor of powers, cut
state budgets). Globovision and Cadena Global under regulatory
pressure. Criminal defamation charges against journalists.
Law against NGOs receiving foreign funding (Law of Political
Sovereignty). Severe_restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009-2010

2010.assessment:
FH CL=6 — significant deterioration. September 2010 elections:
opposition won 47% of vote but only 40% of seats (gerrymandering).
Internet censorship began (CONATEL blocking sites). Judges and
prosecutors facing criminal charges for unfavorable rulings
(Afiuni case). Habilitating law passed December 2010 (decree
powers for 18 months). Civil liberties severely degraded.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010-2011
Human Rights Watch, World Report 2011 - Venezuela (Judge Afiuni)

2011.assessment:
FH CL=6. Chavez diagnosed with cancer (announced June 2011).
Political succession anxiety heightening authoritarianism.
Expanded nationalizations. Opposition Mesa de la Unidad
Democrática (MUD) coalition formed, conducting primaries.
State media dominance; private media self-censoring under
regulatory threat. Internet restrictions increasing.
Severe_restrictions continuing.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011-2012

2012.assessment:
FH CL=6. October 2012: Chavez reelected 55%-44% vs. Capriles.
Competitive election but severe state advantages. December 2012:
Chavez won regional elections. December 2012: Chavez announces
cancer returned (died March 5, 2013). Electoral competition
still occurring but institutional framework systematically
undermined. Severe_restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012-2013

2013.assessment:
FH CL=6. Chavez died March 5, 2013. Maduro won April 2013
presidential election by 1.5% over Capriles — disputed,
no full recount. Maduro government less popular/charismatic
than Chavez. Arrests of opposition figures. Government using
emergency decrees for economic interventions. Political
environment increasingly repressive. Severe_restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013-2014
Human Rights Watch, World Report 2014 - Venezuela

2014.assessment:
FH CL=6. February-July 2014: La Salida mass protests against
Maduro. Government response: 43 killed, 3,000+ arrested,
widespread torture allegations. Opposition leaders Leopoldo
Lopez and Maria Corina Machado jailed or sidelined. Nicolás
Maduro consolidated authoritarian rule. Economy deteriorating
with oil price decline (began mid-2014). Severe_restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014-2015
Human Rights Watch, Punished for Protesting, 2014 (Venezuela)

2015.assessment:
FH CL=7 — maximum restriction level (Not Free). December 2015:
opposition MUD won National Assembly supermajority (112/167
seats). But Supreme Court (TSJ) immediately began blocking
Assembly legislation. Government declared economic emergency;
President Maduro ruling by decree. Leopoldo Lopez in prison;
opposition leaders jailed. Free civil liberties only nominally
exist. No_civil_liberties approaching.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015-2016
Human Rights Watch, World Report 2016 - Venezuela

2016.assessment:
FH CL=7. National Assembly declared in contempt by TSJ;
all Assembly acts annulled. Humanitarian crisis deepening:
food and medicine shortages. Recall referendum against Maduro
blocked by electoral council (CNE). State of emergency
extended repeatedly by decree. Arbitrary detentions of
opposition politicians. Massive emigration beginning.
No_civil_liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016-2017

2017.assessment:
FH CL=7. April-July 2017: massive protests; 120+ killed.
July 2017: Maduro convened Constituent Assembly (ANC) by
decree — elected with opposition boycott, replacing National
Assembly. ANC given unlimited powers. Government declared
opposition political parties without legal standing.
International community (Lima Group, OAS) condemned process.
US targeted sanctions expanded. Complete authoritarian
consolidation. No_civil_liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017-2018
Human Rights Watch, World Report 2018 - Venezuela

2018.assessment:
FH CL=7. May 2018: Maduro "reelected" in sham election
boycotted by major opposition; declared illegitimate by
US, EU, Lima Group. Constituent Assembly held supreme power.
Hyperinflation 65,374%. Economy in freefall. 3M+ Venezuelans
had fled by year-end. Mass arbitrary detentions including
military officers. SEBIN (intelligence) operating death
squads (OAS/HRW documented). No_civil_liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018-2019
OAS General Secretariat, Report on Venezuela, 2018

2019.assessment:
FH CL=7. January 2019: National Assembly President Juan Guaidó
declared himself interim president; recognized by 50+ countries.
Dual government. April 2019: failed uprising attempt (Operacion
Libertad). US comprehensive oil sanctions (January 2019).
Hyperinflation 344,510% (peak). 4M+ refugees. SEBIN and
FAES (special forces) killings documented by UN. Maduro
retained military support; Guaidó authority nominal.
No_civil_liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019-2020
UN Human Rights Council, Report on Venezuela, September 2019

2020.assessment:
FH CL=7. December 2020: National Assembly elections boycotted
by opposition; Maduro allies won nominal majority. TSJ removed
opposition control of major parties. Guaidó's interim government
losing international recognition momentum. COVID used to
restrict movement and assembly. 5M+ refugees. UN fact-finding
mission documented crimes against humanity by Venezuelan
state. No_civil_liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020-2021
UN Fact-Finding Mission on Venezuela, September 2020

2021.assessment:
FH CL=7. November 2021 regional/local elections: opposition
participated in some contests; government claimed legitimacy.
Mexico City negotiations between Maduro and opposition
(suspended). Economy partially stabilizing (dollarization
de facto). Political prisoners: ~240 per Foro Penal. 6M+
refugees. No meaningful civil liberties; arbitrary detention
routine. No_civil_liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021-2022

2022.assessment:
FH CL=7. US-Venezuela backchannel engagement for oil (Russia/
Ukraine war). Chevron license partially restored. Mexico City
talks stalled. Social Control Law passed restricting NGOs
and civil society (CONACUID). Political prisoners at 270+.
Economy stabilizing via dollarization but inequality extreme.
No_civil_liberties; systematic state repression continuing.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022-2023

2023.assessment:
FH CL=7. Barbados Accord (October 2023): Maduro signed agreement
with opposition for free/fair 2024 elections in exchange for
partial US sanctions relief. Opposition held primary (October
2023); Maria Corina Machado won with 92%. Maduro declared
Machado ineligible. 7.7M Venezuelans in exile. Political
prisoners: 300+. No_civil_liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023-2024

2024.assessment:
FH CL=7. July 28, 2024 presidential election: CNE declared
Maduro winner (51%) but refused to publish voting tallies.
Opposition candidate Edmundo González received evidence showing
he won 67%. Massive fraud documented by independent observers.
Post-election crackdown: 2,000+ arrested, 25+ killed.
Maduro declared winner; González fled to Spain asylum.
US, EU, and most LAC countries rejected results.
No_civil_liberties; election fraud at maximum scale.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024

2025.assessment:
FH CL=7. Maduro inaugurated January 10, 2025 for third term
despite disputed election. González continued claiming presidency
from exile. US re-imposed comprehensive sanctions. Arbitrary
detentions continuing; political prisoner count 900+. Civic
space fully closed. No independent judiciary, press, or
assembly rights. No_civil_liberties at maximum severity.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025
Human Rights Watch, World Report 2025 - Venezuela

1995.assessment:
Caldera government. Traditional parties (AD/COPEI) significantly
fragmented after 1992 coup attempts. Caldera won as independent
breakaway from COPEI. Elite cohesion weakened but system
still functioning. Business, military, and political elites
sharing democratic norms though economic crisis causing stress.
Significant_competition_managed: elites competing within
democratic framework.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1995

1996.assessment:
Caldera coalition relatively stable; Agenda Venezuela economic
reforms implemented with business sector cooperation. Chavez
free and organizing but outside formal elite structures.
Traditional party elites beginning to realize their fragmentation
is permanent. Minor_tensions_functional: elites cooperating
on economic stabilization.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1996

1997.assessment:
Pre-election fragmentation intensifying. Traditional AD and COPEI
unable to unite behind single candidate against rising Chavez.
Business elites divided between supporting reform candidate or
backing establishment. Military elites with divided loyalties.
Significant_competition_managed but increasingly toward
open_factional_conflict.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1997

1998.assessment:
Open_factional_conflict. Traditional party elites tried multiple
strategies to stop Chavez (Salas Romer late coalition) but
failed. December 1998: Chavez won 56%. AD and COPEI received
combined under 5%. New chavista coalition vs. discredited
traditional elite. Fundamental elite realignment underway.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1998-1999

1999.assessment:
Active_power_struggles. Chavez forming new chavista elite while
dismantling traditional structures. Constituent assembly process
purged traditional judicial and political elite. Military factions
divided between Chavez loyalists and institutionalists. Business
elite increasingly alarmed. New constitution creating new
institutional order — active reconfiguration of power among
elites.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1999-2000

2000.assessment:
Significant_competition_managed. Chavez consolidated new elite
after mega-elections (July 2000). Chavista bloc internally
unified around Chavez personally. Opposition elites (business/
media/parties) coordinating against government but losing.
Military purged of dissenters; loyalists elevated. Brief
stability as Chavez entrenched new order.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2000-2001

2001.assessment:
Open_factional_conflict. 49 enabling law decrees (November 2001)
triggered unprecedented business-labor-church-media coalition
against Chavez. General strike December 2001. CTV (labor) and
Fedecamaras (business) coalition — traditional rivals united
against Chavez. Chavista elite internally unified but facing
organized systemic opposition from Venezuela's established
institutional elites.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2001-2002

2002.assessment:
Complete_fragmentation. April 2002 coup: military faction
briefly installed Carmona (Fedecamaras president) as president;
Chavez restored by loyal troops after 47 hours. December 2002-
February 2003: oil industry general strike. PDVSA management
vs. Chavez government open warfare. Military split between
pro/anti Chavez. Two parallel power structures in open
confrontation.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2002-2003
Human Rights Watch, World Report 2003 - Venezuela

2003.assessment:
Active_power_struggles. Post-oil-strike: Chavez fired 18,000
PDVSA workers, consolidated oil company under loyalists.
Recall referendum signature collection began. Opposition elites
(Coordinadora Democratica) organizing mass signature campaign.
Tascon List: government using oil company and state employment
to punish opposition signers. Power struggle ongoing but
Chavez gaining upper hand.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2003-2004

2004.assessment:
Significant_competition_managed. Recall referendum (August 2004)
won by Chavez 59%. Opposition elites (Coordinadora Democratica)
fractured after referendum defeat. Chavez consolidated oil
windfall. Chavista elite unified around Chavez; opposition
elites demoralized and reorganizing. Stable dominant chavismo
with managed opposition. TSJ expansion packed courts.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2004-2005

2005.assessment:
Minor_tensions_functional. Chavez at peak domestic power.
Oil boom (~$55/bbl) financing misiones. Opposition boycotted
December 2005 elections — chavistas won 100% of Assembly.
Chavista elite internally coherent with Chavez as undisputed
leader. Internal tensions minor. Military loyalists rewarded.
Near-peak elite cohesion for chavista bloc.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005-2006

2006.assessment:
Minor_tensions_functional. Chavez reelected 63%-44% (December
2006). Declared 21st Century Socialism. Some business elites
who had accommodated moderate chavismo now alarmed. PSUV party
formation (2007) consolidating but some chavista insiders
uncomfortable with radical turn. Opposition elites (Rosales)
competitive but decisively losing. Chavista bloc cohesive.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006-2007

2007.assessment:
Significant_competition_managed. December 2007 constitutional
referendum FAILED 51-49% — Chavez first electoral defeat.
Significant blow to elite cohesion; some chavistas voted
against. RCTV closure alienated moderate allies. Chavez accepted
defeat gracefully but began internal scrutiny of doubters.
Student movement became significant opposition force.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007-2008

2008.assessment:
Significant_competition_managed. Oil at $147 peak; massive
government spending repairing internal chavista cohesion post-
referendum loss. November 2008 regional elections: opposition
won Miranda, Zulia, Carabobo. Chavista bloc recovering cohesion
via oil revenue. No immediate fragmentation risk.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008-2009

2009.assessment:
Minor_tensions_functional. February 2009 indefinite reelection
amendment approved (54%). Chavez consolidated constitutional
path to permanent power. PSUV internally unified around Chavez
personally. Opposition elites increasingly fragmented and
demoralized. Near-peak internal cohesion for chavista bloc.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009-2010

2010.assessment:
Significant_competition_managed. September 2010 elections:
opposition won 47% of vote but only 40% of seats (gerrymandering).
MUD coalition showing competitive strength. Chavez passed
habilitating law (December) for decree powers. Chavista bloc
controlling institutions but facing genuine electoral competition.
Elite competition intensifying.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010-2011

2011.assessment:
Significant_competition_managed. Chavez cancer diagnosis (June
2011) created succession anxiety within chavista elite. Multiple
factions positioning: Maduro, Cabello, Jaua, Flores. Chavez
managing internal succession while maintaining external unified
front. Opposition MUD holding primary. Elite competition within
chavismo intensifying around succession question.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011-2012

2012.assessment:
Significant_competition_managed. Chavez won October 2012
presidential election (55%) vs. Capriles. December: cancer
returned; designated Maduro as successor. Chavista elite
unified behind Chavez-designated successor. Opposition competitive
but losing. Internal chavista succession competition subsumed
by Chavez authority.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012-2013

2013.assessment:
Active_power_struggles. Chavez died March 5, 2013. Maduro vs.
Cabello (Assembly President) for dominant position. Maduro won
April election by 1.5% (disputed). Internal faction fighting:
pragmatic civilians (Maduro) vs. military-connected bolivarian
bourgeoisie (Cabello). Opposition nearly won — putting pressure
on internal elite cohesion. Active power struggles for post-
Chavez leadership.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013-2014

2014.assessment:
Active_power_struggles. La Salida protests (Feb-Jul 2014)
tested Maduro government cohesion. Military factions divided
between hardliners and moderates. Maduro increasingly dependent
on military support (FANB). Cabello controlling National Assembly.
Oil price began declining (mid-2014) reducing patronage.
Economy deteriorating generating internal faction anxiety.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014-2015

2015.assessment:
Open_factional_conflict. December 2015: opposition won National
Assembly supermajority. Maduro-controlled TSJ immediately
blocked Assembly. Maduro vs. Assembly open institutional
conflict. Within chavismo: Maduro vs. military hardliners.
Economic collapse accelerating. Bolichicos (corruption network)
vs. ideological chavistas. Open_factional_conflict within
ruling bloc.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015-2016

2016.assessment:
Open_factional_conflict. Maduro-controlled TSJ annulled all
National Assembly acts. Recall referendum blocked. Economic
collapse (GDP -17%). Military becoming primary governance
institution. Drug trafficking networks within military (Cartel
de los Soles) generating parallel power centers. Maduro
increasingly dependent on narrow military loyalist faction.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016-2017

2017.assessment:
Active_power_struggles. Mass protests Apr-Jul 2017 (120+
killed). Maduro created Constituent Assembly (ANC) July 2017
by decree. ANC given supraconstitutional powers. Military
consolidated as primary state institution. Dissident military
faction coup attempt failed. US targeted sanctions expanded.
Internal chavista factions competing for position in new
ANC order.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017-2018

2018.assessment:
Active_power_struggles. Sham election May 2018; Maduro
consolidated civilian-military elite. August 2018: assassination
