within FANB. Maduro survived; purged military officers.
Hyperinflation destroying state employee patronage. Competing
factions fighting for remaining resources in collapsing economy.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018-2019

2019.assessment:
Complete_fragmentation. January 2019: Guaidó declared interim
president recognized by 50+ countries. Two parallel governments:
Maduro (control of military/territory) vs. Guaidó (international
recognition/National Assembly). April 2019 uprising failed.
Military remained with Maduro. Complete fragmentation of
Venezuelan state legitimacy.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019-2020

2020.assessment:
Active_power_struggles. Guaidó parallel government losing
steam. Maduro strengthened control with TSJ removing opposition-

Colombian FARC/ELN present in border states. Cartel de los

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020-2021

2021.assessment:
Significant_competition_managed. Maduro stabilizing through
dollarization and oil export recovery. Mexico City negotiations

Guaidó parallel government fading. Maduro consolidating within

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021-2022

2022.assessment:
Significant_competition_managed. Maduro ruling coalition

Internal military-civilian factions managed via limited
oil revenue recovery patronage.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022-2023

2023.assessment:
Significant_competition_managed. Barbados Accord (October 2023)

business/military faction aligned with negotiations.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023-2024

2024.assessment:
Active_power_struggles. July 28, 2024 election: massive fraud;
CNE claimed Maduro won without publishing tallies; evidence

Maduro surviving through military loyalty despite international
condemnation. Internal chavista elite purges suspected. Active
power struggle between Maduro survival faction and broader
Venezuelan society.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024

2025.assessment:
Active_power_struggles. Maduro inaugurated January 10, 2025
amid protests and international rejection. 900+ political
prisoners. González claiming presidency from Spain. Machado
underground in Venezuela. Maduro narrow military-loyalist
elite maintaining control through repression. FARC/ELN presence
in border regions represents alternative power centers.
Survival governance by narrow elite faction.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025

Functional_but_flawed. Democratic Venezuela under Caldera.
Congress, courts, elections all functioning. Serious

democratic functions operating: competitive elections,
legislative oversight, judicial review. Functional_but_flawed.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1995

1996.assessment:
Functional_but_flawed. Caldera government; Congress,

Functional_but_flawed. Pre-election year. Institutions

institutional reform. No independent judiciary, legislature,
or electoral body. No_functioning_institutions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022-2023

2023.assessment:
No_functioning_institutions. Barbados Accord created
appearance of negotiated electoral opening but CNE remained
under Maduro control. TSJ declared Machado ineligible
without legal basis. Electoral institutions nominally
conducting processes but without independence.
No_functioning_institutions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023-2024

2024.assessment:
No_functioning_institutions. July 28 presidential election:
CNE declared Maduro winner without publishing voting tallies.
Carter Center declared election not meeting democratic
standards. TSJ validated Maduro "victory" without evidence.
Electoral institutions completely captured. Legislature rubber
stamp. Post-election crackdown showing no rule of law.
No_functioning_institutions.

sources.citation: Carter Center Venezuela Election Report 2024

2025.assessment:
No_functioning_institutions. Maduro inaugurated January
10, 2025 in disputed ceremony. Assembly functioning as
rubber stamp. TSJ subordinate to executive. CNE captured.
Armed forces loyal to Maduro. PDVSA instrument of state.
All institutions without independence or democratic function.
No_functioning_institutions at maximum degree.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025

1995.assessment:
Low_100_500. Democratic Venezuela under Caldera. No active
armed conflict. High homicide rate from criminal violence
(already one of highest in world) but low political violence
specifically. Some clashes with guerrilla groups near Colombia
border. No organized political violence against state.
Estimated ~150 deaths from political/conflict violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset, Venezuela historical

1996.assessment:
Low_100_500. Caldera government. No political conflict.
Some border incidents with FARC/ELN. Criminal violence
high but political violence low. Democratic system
functioning. ~150 estimated political violence deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

1997.assessment:
Low_100_500. Pre-election period. Political competition
electoral not violent. Some Colombia border incidents.
Criminal violence high but political violence minimal.
~150 estimated political violence deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

1998.assessment:
Low_100_500 / minimal_lt_100. Election year with peaceful
electoral competition. Chavez won December 1998 in
free and fair election without violence. Lowest political
violence of the period. ~100 estimated political conflict
deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

1999.assessment:
Low_100_500 / minimal. Chavez inaugurated February 1999.
Massive political transformation through referendum/constituent
assembly but without significant violence. December 1999:
floods and landslides killed 30,000+ (natural disaster, not
political). Political transition peaceful.
~100 estimated political violence deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

2000.assessment:
Low_100_500. Mega-elections July 2000. Some political
violence between chavista colectivos and opposition supporters.
Criminal violence accelerating (homicides rising steeply).
No organized armed conflict. ~200 estimated political
violence deaths including state repression incidents.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

2001.assessment:
Low_100_500. 49 decree laws + December 2001 general strike.
Political tensions very high but violence limited. Opposition
organizing through strikes and protests not armed conflict.
Colectivo intimidation incidents. ~200 estimated political
violence deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

2002.assessment:
Low_100_500 / minimal. April 11-12, 2002: ~19 killed during
coup attempt (both pro and anti-Chavez). December 2002 oil
strike: some violent incidents. Surprisingly limited deaths
given massive political confrontation. Minimal_lt_100 to
Low_100_500 borderline. ~60 estimated direct political
violence deaths (not counting full coup/strike period).

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, World Report 2003 - Venezuela (coup deaths)

2003.assessment:
Low_100_500. Post-oil-strike consolidation; Tascon List
discrimination. Colectivo intimidation continuing. Some
political killings of opposition figures. Recall referendum
petition collection targeted. Criminal violence very high
(homicides 10,000+/year) but political violence specifically
low. ~100 estimated political violence deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

2004.assessment:
Low_100_500. Recall referendum (August 2004) — some pre/post
election violence. Opposition disputed results; protests
dispersed. Colectivo intimidation of opposition voters
documented. Criminal violence at 10,000+/year (homicides)
but distinct from political violence. ~100 political violence
deaths estimated.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

2005.assessment:
Low_100_500 / minimal. Oil boom funding misiones; population
relatively pacified. Opposition boycotted elections so no
electoral violence. Colectivo activity lower. Low point
of political violence in mid-Chavez era. ~100 estimated
political violence deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

2006.assessment:
Low_100_500. Chavez reelected December 2006 in competitive
but relatively peaceful election. Some election-related
violence. Colectivo activity. Criminal violence very high
(homicides 12,000+/year) separate from political violence.
~100 estimated political violence deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

2007.assessment:
Low_100_500. RCTV shutdown (May) triggered student protests;
several deaths. December 2007 constitutional referendum
campaign tense but relatively peaceful despite narrow loss.
Colectivo-student confrontations. ~100 estimated political
violence deaths including student protest casualties.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, A Decade Under Chavez, 2008

2008.assessment:
Low_100_500. Regional elections November 2008; some
violence. Oil at $147/bbl peak — social programs limiting
political discontent. Criminal violence at historic highs
(homicides 14,000+/year) but distinct from political.
~100 political violence deaths estimated.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

2009.assessment:
Low_100_500. Indefinite reelection approved February 2009.
Referendum campaign relatively peaceful. Opposition
demoralized. Colectivo activity continuing. Criminal
violence at 16,000+/year homicides. ~100 political
violence deaths estimated.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

2010.assessment:
Low_100_500. September 2010 elections competitive; some
political violence around electoral campaign. Colectivo
intimidation of opposition supporters documented. Criminal
violence at 17,000+/year (homicides). Political violence
increasing as opposition competitive again. ~150 estimated
political violence deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

2011.assessment:
Low_100_500. Chavez cancer diagnosis; succession anxiety.
Colectivo activity elevated. Political persecution of
opposition judges (Afiuni). Criminal violence at 19,000+/year
homicides. Political violence specifically ~150 deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

2012.assessment:
Low_100_500. October 2012 presidential election — competitive
campaign with colectivo violence against opposition. Post-
election violence limited. Some deaths during campaign.
Chavez reelected. Criminal homicides 21,000+/year. ~200
estimated political violence deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

2013.assessment:
Low_100_500. Chavez died March 5. Maduro election April 14
(1.5% margin) — post-election violence against opposition
demanding recount. ~11 killed in post-election clashes.
Colectivos attacking opposition centers. ~200 estimated
political violence deaths including post-election killings.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, World Report 2014 - Venezuela (post-election violence)

2014.assessment:
Low_100_500. La Salida mass protests February-July 2014:
43 killed (government security forces and colectivos vs.
protesters). 3,000+ arrested. Widespread torture documented
by HRW and Amnesty. Colectivos (motorcycles) used to
disperse/attack protests. Transition from low to escalating
political violence. Documented 43 deaths; actual total
likely higher including criminal/political hybrid violence.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Punished for Protesting, May 2014
Amnesty International, Venezuela: Rule by Decree, 2014

2015.assessment:
Moderate_500_2000 / Low_100_500. Economic crisis accelerating.
December 2015 election: opposition won 112/167 seats despite
colectivo intimidation. State violence against protesters
continuing. SEBIN (intelligence) arbitrary detentions.
Opposition leaders jailed. Colombia border region: guerrilla
violence. Estimated ~300 political violence deaths in 2015.

sources.citation: ACLED Venezuela dataset 2015

2016.assessment:
Moderate_500_2000. GDP -17%; severe shortages triggering
social unrest. OLP (Operación de Liberación del Pueblo):
government anti-crime operations killing hundreds extrajudicially
in poor barrios. Colectivos attacking opposition. SEBIN
arbitrary detentions escalating. Recall referendum organizers
attacked. Transitioning to moderate political violence.
~500 estimated political/state violence deaths.

sources.citation: ACLED Venezuela dataset 2016
Human Rights Watch, World Report 2017 - Venezuela (OLP operations)

2017.assessment:
Moderate_500_2000. April-July 2017 mass protests: 124 killed
(documented by OHCHR). Thousands injured and detained. ANC
created by decree July 2017 bypassing National Assembly.
FAES (special forces) killings in poor barrios ongoing
throughout year. Total political and state violence deaths
estimated 500+. OHCHR report condemned government actions.

sources.citation: OHCHR Report on Venezuela, August 2017
Human Rights Watch, World Report 2018 - Venezuela

2018.assessment:
High_2000_10000. OHCHR documented 5,287 killed by FAES
in 2018 for "resistance to authority" — systematic extrajudicial
killings in poor barrios. Plus protest deaths, political
detentions with torture (SEBIN). UN fact-finding mission
later found these constitute crimes against humanity.
Transition to high political/state violence level.

sources.citation: OHCHR Report on Venezuela, July 2019 (2018 FAES data)
UN Fact-Finding Mission on Venezuela, September 2020

2019.assessment:
High_2000_10000. OHCHR documented 5,286 FAES killings
in first five months of 2019. April 2019 Operacion Libertad
uprising: ~5 additional deaths. Systematic extrajudicial
killings in poor barrios as government anti-crime operations.
4M+ refugees fled to avoid repression and economic collapse.
UN fact-finding commission initiated.

sources.citation: OHCHR Report on Venezuela, July 2019

2020.assessment:
High_2000_10000 / Moderate_500_2000. COVID reduced some FAES
operational tempo. UN FFM report (September 2020) documented
crimes against humanity for 2018-2019 killings. FAES killings
continuing but at reduced rate. Border violence with Colombia
(FARC/ELN). Operation Gedeon failed mercenary incursion (May
2020). ~2,000 estimated political/state violence deaths.

sources.citation: UN Fact-Finding Mission on Venezuela, September 2020

2021.assessment:
Moderate_500_2000. March-April 2021 Operation Escudo
Bolivariano in Apure (vs. dissident FARC): significant
military engagement; dozens killed; thousands displaced.
FAES killings in urban areas continuing at lower rate
than 2018-2019 peaks. Moderate political/state violence.
~1,000 estimated deaths.

sources.citation: ACLED Venezuela 2021
International Crisis Group, Venezuela: Containing the Armed Groups, 2021

2022.assessment:
Moderate_500_2000. FAES operations continuing at reduced
pace. FARC/ELN border violence continuing. Colectivo activity
in cities. Economy partially stabilizing (dollarization) but
political violence continuing from security force operations.
OHCHR monitoring. ~800 estimated political/state violence
deaths.

sources.citation: ACLED Venezuela 2022

2023.assessment:
Moderate_500_2000. Pre-election year; political targeting
of opposition activists. FAES continuing security operations.
FARC/ELN border violence. Barbados Accord created brief
opening but political violence continuing. Tren de Aragua
territorial violence. ~700 estimated political/state
violence deaths.

sources.citation: ACLED Venezuela 2023

2024.assessment:
Moderate_500_2000. July 28, 2024 election and aftermath:
25+ killed in post-election crackdown; 2,000+ detained.
Pre-election: opposition activists detained, threatened.
FAES continuing throughout year. FARC/ELN border violence.
Post-election crackdown most acute episode. Total year
~800 estimated political/state violence deaths.

sources.citation: Carter Center, Venezuela Election Final Report 2024
Human Rights Watch, Venezuela Post-Election Crackdown 2024

2025.assessment:
Moderate_500_2000. Maduro inaugurated January 10, 2025
amid ongoing crackdown. Political prisoners 900+. Continued
FAES security operations. FARC/ELN border violence.
Colectivo activity in urban areas. Sustained level of
moderate political/state violence. ~500 estimated for 2025
(data not yet fully available).

sources.citation: ACLED Venezuela (2025 ongoing)

1995.assessment:
Full central government control of national territory. No active
insurgencies. Colombia border area with some guerrilla presence
(FARC/ELN spillover) but Venezuelan state authority maintained.
Military conducting routine operations. Democratic government
under Caldera with effective territorial administration.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1995

1996.assessment:
Full central government territorial control. Colombian border
areas experience some FARC/ELN activity but Venezuelan state
maintains control. Economic crisis (banking collapse 1994)
not affecting territorial integrity. Full_control_no_challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1996

1997.assessment:
Full territorial control. Pre-election period; no territorial
challenges. Colombian border guerrilla presence minor and
contained. Venezuelan armed forces (FANB) effectively
controlling national territory. Full_control_no_challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1997

1998.assessment:
Full territorial control. Chavez elected December 1998 within
democratic framework. No territorial insurgencies or contested
areas. Colombian border minor guerrilla activity remains but
Venezuelan state authority uncontested. Full_control_no_challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1998-1999

1999.assessment:
Full territorial control. Chavez inaugurated; constituent
assembly; new constitution. Military fully under Chavez
command. No territorial challenges despite massive
political transformation. Full_control_no_challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1999-2000

2000.assessment:
Full territorial control. Mega-elections July 2000; Chavez
consolidated government. Military loyalty ensured. No
separatist movements; no significant insurgencies. Colombia
border guerrilla activity minor. Full_control_no_challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2000-2001

2001.assessment:
Full territorial control. 49 enabling law decrees creating
political opposition but no territorial fracture. Armed
forces loyal to Chavez. Opposition organizing through
strikes not territorial control challenges.
Full_control_no_challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2001-2002

2002.assessment:
Minor_insurgencies_lt_10pct / full control — temporary loss
during April 2002 coup (48 hours). April 11-13: Carmona
briefly controlled government; Chavez restored. No prolonged
territorial fracture. December 2002 oil strike: economic
disruption but not territorial control loss. Full control
restored after coup. Full_control_no_challenges (post-coup).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2002-2003

2003.assessment:
Full territorial control. Post-oil-strike Chavez consolidated
PDVSA and state. Military fully aligned with Chavez. No
territorial challenges. Colombia border minor guerrilla
activity. Full_control_no_challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2003-2004

2004.assessment:
Full territorial control. Recall referendum won; Chavez
consolidated control. FARC/ELN border cooperation with
Venezuela reportedly increasing but Venezuelan state authority
uncontested. Full_control_no_challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2004-2005

2005.assessment:
Full territorial control. Oil boom peak; misiones expanding.
Opposition boycotted elections; Chavez 100% Assembly. FANB
fully loyal. No territorial challenges whatsoever.
Full_control_no_challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005-2006

2006.assessment:
Full territorial control. Chavez reelected 63%. Oil boom.
Military loyal. No separatist movements or insurgencies.
FARC/ELN border presence contained. Full_control_no_challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006-2007

2007.assessment:
Full territorial control. RCTV shutdown; constitutional
referendum failed (Dec 2007). Military loyal despite
referendum loss. No territorial challenges. FARC/ELN border
activity: Chavez sympathetic but Venezuelan control uncontested.
Full_control_no_challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007-2008

2008.assessment:
Full territorial control. Oil at $147 peak; massive state
investment. Military loyal. Opposition won regional elections
but through democratic process, not territorial contestation.
Full_control_no_challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008-2009

2009.assessment:
Full territorial control. Indefinite reelection approved;
Chavez government entrenched. Military fully loyal. No
territorial challenges. FARC/ELN border presence acknowledged
but contained. Full_control_no_challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009-2010

2010.assessment:
Full territorial control. Elections held; Chavez retained
Assembly control. FARC/ELN presence in Apure, Zulia states
increasing but not contesting sovereignty. Military loyal.
Full_control_no_challenges with minor caveat for border areas.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010-2011

2011.assessment:
Full territorial control. Chavez cancer but military loyal.
FARC/ELN border presence in Apure/Zulia recognized but not
challenging Venezuelan sovereignty. State maintains administrative
control of all 23 states. Full_control_no_challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011-2012

2012.assessment:
Full territorial control. Chavez reelected; military unified.
FARC/ELN border presence but not challenging Venezuelan
sovereignty. All administrative territories under government
control. Full_control_no_challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012-2013

2013.assessment:
Full territorial control. Chavez died; Maduro narrowly elected.
Military remained loyal to Maduro (Chavez choice). FARC/ELN
border presence continuing. PRAN prison gangs controlling
prisons (de facto prison non-control). Full territorial
control outside prisons. Full_control_no_challenges for
formal territory.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013-2014

2014.assessment:
Minor_insurgencies_lt_10pct. La Salida mass protests Feb-Jul
2014 but government restored control through force. FARC/ELN
border areas increasingly autonomous. PRAN prison gangs
controlling prison facilities entirely. Colectivos (armed
pro-government groups) effective parallel security in
barrios. State losing monopoly on violence in periphery.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Punished for Protesting, 2014

2015.assessment:
Minor_insurgencies_lt_10pct. Economic collapse accelerating;
security deteriorating. FARC/ELN presence in Apure, Zulia,
Amazonas expanding. Illegal mining (garimpeiros) in Bolivar
state expanding beyond state control. Colectivos operating
as parallel security. Government controls formal
administrative structures but peripheral areas losing state
presence.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015-2016
International Crisis Group, Venezuela: Containing the Chaos, 2017

2016.assessment:
Minor_insurgencies_lt_10pct. Severe economic collapse (GDP -17%).
Security forces strained. FARC/ELN presence in border states
consolidating. Arco Minero del Orinoco decreed (February 2016):
mining rights in 12% of national territory creating ungoverned
extractive zones. Colectivos effectively policing urban
barrios instead of formal security forces.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Venezuela: Containing the Chaos, 2017

2017.assessment:
Minor_insurgencies_lt_10pct. 120+ killed in mass protests but
government retained territorial control through force. FARC/ELN
significantly expanded in border states (Apure). Peace deal
in Colombia (2016) released FARC capacity to expand into
Venezuela. Illegal mining (coltan, gold) in southern states
partially outside state control. Government retaining core
urban/administrative control.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Venezuela: Containing the Chaos, 2017

2018.assessment:
Significant_areas_outside_10_30pct. FARC/ELN effectively
controlling significant portions of Apure state and parts of
Zulia, Bolivar, Amazonas. Illegal mining syndicates (pranes)
controlling mining zones in Arc Minero. Maduro government
accommodating rather than contesting these parallel powers.
Major cities and administrative centers remain under nominal
government control.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Gold and Grief in Venezuela's Violent South, 2019
InSight Crime, Venezuela 2018 Report

2019.assessment:
Significant_areas_outside_10_30pct. FARC/ELN consolidated
in Apure, parts of Zulia and Amazonas. Dual government
situation (Maduro vs. Guaidó) created confusion about
administrative control in some regions. SEBIN/FANB operations
in mining areas but not establishing full control. Colectivos
substitute for police in many urban areas. Significant portions
of territory with multiple_competing_authorities.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Gold and Grief in Venezuela's Violent South, 2019

2020.assessment:
Significant_areas_outside_10_30pct. COVID used to restrict
movement. FARC/ELN continued consolidating control in border
regions. May 2020: failed mercenary incursion (Operation
Gedeon) from Colombia — embarrassing but quickly suppressed.
Colectivos as parallel security forces in urban barrios.
Maduro retaining control of administrative centers but
peripheral areas largely ungoverned.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Venezuela 2020

2021.assessment:
Significant_areas_outside_10_30pct. March-April 2021: Maduro
launched Operation Escudo Bolivariano in Apure against
Colombian dissident FARC group — major military operation
indicating FARC had established significant territorial
presence. Thousands of Venezuelans fled to Colombia (internal
displacement). FARC/ELN present in multiple border states.
South and west Venezuela substantially outside effective
government control.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group, Venezuela: Containing the Armed Groups, 2021

2022.assessment:
Significant_areas_outside_10_30pct. FARC/ELN presence in
Apure, Zulia, Bolivar, Amazonas states continuing. Illegal
gold and coltan mining syndicates controlling extractive
zones in south. Colectivos operating as parallel security in
major cities. Government nominally controls administrative
centers but peripheral sovereignty severely compromised.

sources.citation: InSight Crime, Venezuela Crime Profile 2022

2023.assessment:
Significant_areas_outside_10_30pct. Barbados Accord implicitly
acknowledged Venezuela's internal security situation. FARC/ELN
continued territorial presence. Tren de Aragua criminal
organization expanding from prisons into territory in multiple
states and internationally. Government unable or unwilling to
control these armed groups. Significant territorial fragmentation.

sources.citation: InSight Crime, Venezuela Organized Crime Landscape 2023

2024.assessment:
Significant_areas_outside_10_30pct. Post-election crisis
(July 28) demonstrated government control through repression
rather than legitimacy. FARC/ELN continued presence in
border states. Tren de Aragua declared terrorist organization
by US (2024). Colectivos active in post-election crackdown
alongside security forces. Government retaining urban
administrative control through force.

sources.citation: InSight Crime, Venezuela 2024

2025.assessment:
Significant_areas_outside_10_30pct. Maduro inaugurated amid
repression. FARC/ELN and criminal organizations continuing
territorial presence in border and southern states. Government
retains control of Caracas and main administrative centers.
Significant portions of territory (Apure, Bolivar, Zulia
border areas, Amazonas) effectively outside central
government control.

sources.citation: InSight Crime, Venezuela 2025

1995.assessment:
Minimal budget transparency under Caldera. Budget documents produced but public
accessibility very limited. Oil revenues managed through PDVSA with limited
congressional oversight. FIEM (Macroeconomic Stabilization Fund) being discussed.
Venezuela had functioning congressional oversight in principle but executive
control of oil revenues opaque. IBP survey not yet established; estimated
low by modern standards. Some fiscal reporting to IMF/World Bank.

sources.citation: Estimate based on Caldera-era governance records; pre-IBP era

1996.assessment:
Minimal budget transparency. FIEM oil stabilization fund established but
management opaque. Currency stabilization (Agenda Venezuela) improved some
macroeconomic reporting. Budget documents produced but limited public access.
Oil sector fiscal transfers to government not fully disclosed.

sources.citation: Estimate pre-IBP era

1997.assessment:
Minimal transparency. PDVSA financial statements produced but not publicly
accessible in meaningful form. Congressional oversight limited despite formal
democratic structures. Budget approved by Congress but executive supplemental
spending through oil revenues largely opaque.

sources.citation: Estimate pre-IBP era

1998.assessment:
Minimal transparency. Transition year; Chavez elected December 1998. Budget
documents produced under Caldera. Some improvement in macroeconomic reporting
from IMF engagement. Oil revenues through FIEM partially disclosed. Pre-Chavez
fiscal management slightly more transparent than what followed.

sources.citation: Estimate pre-IBP era

1999.assessment:
Scant transparency. Chavez constituent assembly (1999 constitution) eliminated
old fiscal oversight mechanisms before new ones established. PDVSA revenues
increasingly directed to social programs off-budget. Congressional oversight
disrupted by constitutional transition. Enabling laws beginning to concentrate
fiscal authority in executive. GDP contraction (-6%) compressing budget.

sources.citation: Estimate; Chavez fiscal centralization literature

2000.assessment:
Scant transparency. New 1999 constitution nominally provided for Controllership
but executive increasingly managing PDVSA revenues directly. Social missions
funded through PDVSA bypassing national budget. Congressional role in budget
oversight diminished. Misiones social programs operated as parallel fiscal system.

sources.citation: Estimate; academic literature on Venezuela fiscal opacity

2001.assessment:
Scant transparency. Enabling law (November 2001) gave Chavez decree powers
bypassing congressional budget oversight. PDVSA financial transfers to social
missions not appearing in official budget. FIEM raided by presidential decree.
Oil windfall profits increasingly managed outside normal fiscal framework.

sources.citation: Estimate; enabling law fiscal analysis

2002.assessment:
Very low transparency. Coup (April 2002) and oil strike (December 2002) disrupted
fiscal planning entirely. PDVSA revenues collapsed during strike. Emergency fiscal
measures without transparency. Currency controls (CADIVI, February 2003) eliminating
market-based fiscal signals. Budget process effectively suspended during crisis.

sources.citation: Estimate; coup/strike fiscal disruption literature

2003.assessment:
Very low transparency. Post-strike recovery; PDVSA fully restructured under
government control after firing 18,000 workers. PDVSA financial statements
became classified/non-disclosed. Budget supplemental spending through PDVSA
social fund (FONDESPA) entirely opaque. IMF consultation data minimal.

sources.citation: Estimate; post-PDVSA restructuring transparency analysis

2004.assessment:
Very low transparency. Oil price boom generating massive windfall revenues
managed entirely outside budget through PDVSA social fund and FONDEN (National
Development Fund, established 2005). Recall referendum won; political stability
allowed entrenchment of parallel fiscal system. Budget only showed fraction
of actual government spending.

sources.citation: IBP preliminary surveys; academic analysis of Venezuela fiscal opacity

2005.assessment:
Very low transparency. FONDEN established (National Development Fund) to manage
excess oil revenues outside budget. FONDEN received $6B+ from PDVSA 2005-2006,
spending entirely without congressional approval or public disclosure. Misiones
social spending via PDVSA and FONDEN estimated at 30-40% of government spending
but invisible in official budget.

sources.citation: Estimate; FONDEN analysis literature

2006.assessment:
First IBP OBS measurement. Score 11/100 confirms very low transparency.
FONDEN managing billions in oil revenues without disclosure. PDVSA social
fund (FONDESPA) untransparent. Budget documents produced but incomplete;
off-budget spending massive. No independent audit institution with real
power. Supreme Audit institution (Contraloria) government-controlled.
Citizens have no meaningful access to budget information.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership Open Budget Survey 2006

2007.assessment:
Very low transparency. CANTV, Orinoco belt nationalizations requiring large
fiscal transfers not disclosed publicly. FONDEN receiving record transfers.
Constituent assembly (December 2007 referendum defeated) fiscal implications
opaque. Between 2006 and 2008 IBP surveys, slight changes in budget document
publication but fundamental opacity unchanged.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2008 interpolated; academic analysis

2008.assessment:
IBP OBS 2008: 25/100 — minimal/scant border. Unusual improvement from 2006 (11)
likely reflects publication of more pre-budget statements and audit reports,
not fundamental improvement in oil revenue transparency. FONDEN and PDVSA
social spending still opaque. Mid-year report published. Off-budget oil
revenues estimated at $30B+ for 2008. Score reflects document publication
not actual accountability.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership Open Budget Survey 2008

2009.assessment:
Very low transparency. Oil price crash reducing revenues disclosed in FONDEN
but emergency spending opaque. GDP recession -3.2%. Additional nationalizations
(steel, banking) without transparent fiscal accounting. Between surveys;
transparency deteriorating back toward 2006 levels.

sources.citation: IBP OBS interpolated; academic analysis

2010.assessment:
IBP OBS 2010: 11/100. Returned to 2006-level opacity. Economic crisis 2009
led to reduced budget document publication. Off-budget spending through FONDEN,
BANDES (development bank), social missions consuming majority of public resources
outside formal budget. Executive consolidated control; no independent fiscal
oversight institution functioning. Citizens have near-zero access to fiscal data.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership Open Budget Survey 2010

2011.assessment:
Very low transparency. Oil revenues high but managed through opaque channels.
PDVSA financial statements minimal disclosure. China-Venezuela oil-for-loans
funds ($20B+) managed entirely outside formal budget. Gran Mision Vivienda
housing program billion-dollar spending not in official budget.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2012 interpolated

2012.assessment:
IBP OBS 2012: 10/100. Extremely low. Consecutive measurement confirming
near-complete budget opacity. Off-budget spending (FONDEN, PDVSA social fund,
BANDES, Gran Mision Vivienda) constituting estimated 50-60% of total public

Under Caldera apertura petrolera, PDVSA published some financial disclosures

data. Apertura contracts registered publicly. Oil revenue transfers to government
budget partially disclosed. Better transparency than post-2003 but still limited

judicial corruption undermining predictability. Estimated WJP Factor 3 ~0.36.

WJP ~0.22. No accessible framework. Dual government crisis (January 2019):
Guaido declared acting president; TSJ declared him illegal; 50+ countries
recognized Guaido. Two competing legal orders with neither functioning as
accessible legal framework. SEBIN and DGCIM (military intelligence) detaining
political opponents outside any legal process. UN OHCHR documented systematic
violations. No FOI, no due process, no independent courts.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2019, Factor 3

2020.assessment:
WJP ~0.22. No accessible framework. COVID pandemic used to expand emergency
powers decree without legal limits. Arbitrary detention of quarantine violators.
NGOs and independent legal organizations harassed and criminalized. Law on
Foreign Agents (2010) used against civil society organizations. No functioning
legal recourse for anyone opposing government. International Criminal Court
(ICC) opened preliminary investigation into Venezuela crimes against humanity.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2020, Factor 3

2021.assessment:
WJP ~0.22. No accessible legal framework. ANC dissolved (January 2021)—ironically
removing even that pseudo-legal body. Maduro National Assembly (elected December
2020 in boycotted election) claiming full legislative authority. New Assembly
passing laws with no transparency. Guaido's parallel government losing
international support. ICC formal investigation opened November 2021. UN FFM
(Fact-Finding Mission) documenting crimes against humanity.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2021, Factor 3

2022.assessment:
WJP ~0.22. No accessible framework. Dollarization enabling limited economic
activity but no legal improvement. NGO law restrictions tightened (SAREN registry
requirements). Barbados Accord preliminary talks beginning but no legal
concessions. Political prisoners including Freddy Superlano, Maria Corina
Machado persecuted. ICC investigation active. No meaningful rule of law.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2022, Factor 3

2023.assessment:
WJP ~0.22. No accessible legal framework. Barbados Accord (October 2023)
included electoral guarantees on paper but legal framework unchanged. Maria
Corina Machado won primary (October 2023) but disqualified by TSJ from running
in elections. Legal framework used to deny electoral participation. No FOI law.
No due process for political opponents. ICC investigation continuing.

sources.citation: World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2023, Factor 3

2024.assessment:
No accessible framework, deteriorating. July 2024 election: Maduro declared
winner without disclosing tally sheets (actas); TSJ validated results without
review. Hundreds of opposition activists, witnesses, and journalists arrested
post-election. Gonzalez Urrutia forced into exile. Edmundo Gonzalez campaign
prosecuted. Any legal challenge to election results immediately dismissed by
captured courts. Near-complete annihilation of legal framework.

sources.citation: WJP 2024 estimate; post-election legal analysis

2025.assessment:
No accessible legal framework projected. Post-election authoritarian consolidation.
Courts fully serving political function. Political prisoners continuing to be
held. No FOI law. No due process. Anti-Hate Law and Bolivarian Intelligence
SEBIN/DGCIM operating as extrajudicial instruments. ICC investigation providing
no immediate protection for citizens. Venezuela among bottom 5 globally on
WJP open government factor. No prospect of improvement without political change.

sources.citation: WJP 2025 projection; ICC investigation status

1995.assessment:
Problematic but not severely repressive press environment under Caldera. Private
commercial TV (Venevision, RCTV, Televen) operating independently. Major newspapers
(El Universal, El Nacional) published critically. Some government advertising pressure
on media. Journalists covering guerrilla activity or drug trafficking faced some
risks. CPJ documented occasional harassment. Venezuela considered one of better
press freedom environments in South America at this time. Estimated RSF raw ~32.

sources.citation: CPJ Venezuela annual reports 1995; Freedom House Freedom of Press 1995

1996.assessment:
Problematic press environment. Private media operating with some restrictions.
Currency crisis generating economic pressure on media (reduced advertising).
Government advertising used as leverage. Journalists investigating corruption
faced pressure. Overall still relatively open by regional standards.

sources.citation: CPJ Venezuela 1996; Freedom House

1997.assessment:
Problematic but relatively open. Private media vigorous. Political campaigns
(Chavez 1998 presidential campaign) covered openly. Oil sector and business
press independent. Slight improvement: political opening around elections
generated media activity. CPJ noted some harassment of reporters covering
crime/military but overall reasonable press environment.

sources.citation: CPJ Venezuela 1997; Freedom House

1998.assessment:
Problematic. Election year; media actively covering Chavez campaign and opposition.
Private TV highly influential in 1998 elections. Chavez's anti-media rhetoric
during campaign beginning to signal future tensions. Overall pre-election media
environment active and relatively free. Some journalists reporting threats
covering crime and political violence.

sources.citation: CPJ Venezuela 1998; Freedom House

1999.assessment:
Deteriorating press environment. Chavez inaugural rhetoric denouncing media as
oligarchic enemies. Constituent assembly process; media critical of Chavez process
facing verbal attacks by president. No formal censorship yet but self-censorship
increasing. RCTV, Globovision, Venevision critical of Chavez; government
responding with verbal attacks. CPJ documented verbal threats but not imprisonment.
Estimated RSF raw ~38 (problematic moving toward difficult).

sources.citation: CPJ Venezuela 1999 annual report; Freedom House 1999

2000.assessment:
Difficult press environment emerging. Chavez verbal attacks on media intensifying.
Broadcast media (RCTV, Globovision, Venevision) openly hostile to Chavez and
covered opposition extensively. Government threatening regulatory action.
CPJ documented journalist harassment. Law on Responsabilidad Social en Radio
y Television (RESORTE) being discussed. Print media more independent but
facing advertising pressure.

sources.citation: CPJ Venezuela 2000 annual report; Freedom House 2000

2001.assessment:
Difficult and deteriorating. Enabling laws (November 2001) threatening media
ownership and broadcast rights. Private TV networks covering anti-government
protests and strikes in detail. Chavez labeling TV networks as coup-plotting
enemies. Enabling law included provisions affecting broadcast licensing.
Journalists covering protests reporting physical threats from pro-Chavez groups.

sources.citation: CPJ Venezuela 2001; Freedom House

2002.assessment:
First RSF measurement year: Very serious. Private TV networks (RCTV, Globovision,
Venevision, Televen) broadcast coup (April 2002) and counter-coup; accused of
facilitating coup by Chavez supporters. Chavez government accused private media
of coup conspiracy. Post-coup: government threatened broadcast license revocations.
Violence against journalists during April events (some injured). Press freedom
situation among most polarized in hemisphere. RSF ranked Venezuela highly problematic.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2002; CPJ Venezuela 2002

2003.assessment:
Very serious. Post-oil-strike media environment extremely hostile. RESORTE
broadcast law passed (December 2004, being drafted). PDVSA government reinstated
after firing 18,000 workers; media coverage hostile. CPJ documented journalist
arrests and threats during oil strike. Pro-government colectivos confronting
journalists. Government advertising withdrawal as punishment for critical coverage.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2003; CPJ Venezuela 2003

2004.assessment:
Very serious. RESORTE law passed: required broadcasters to carry mandatory
government programming (cadenas) and restricted content. Recall referendum
(August 2004); private media covered opposition campaign but under regulatory
pressure. New criminal defamation provisions (vilipendio laws) threatening
journalists who criticized Maduro. Several journalists arrested or fined.
CPJ documented multiple cases of journalist harassment and prosecution.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2004; CPJ Venezuela 2004

2005.assessment:
Very serious. Criminal defamation (contempt law) used against journalists.
Government advertising used to starve critical media financially. Conatel
(regulator) monitoring content and threatening licenses. CPJ reported multiple
imprisonments or threats. Private TV still operating but under severe regulatory
and financial pressure. Community radio network (pro-government) expanded.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2005; CPJ Venezuela 2005

2006.assessment:
Very serious. Chavez re-election campaign; media critical of Chavez under
regulatory and financial pressure. RCTV case: government announced non-renewal
of RCTV broadcast license (to expire May 2007). RCTV closure announced as
punishment for coup involvement alleged in 2002. Journalists facing criminal
prosecution for defamation. Self-censorship pervasive in broadcast sector.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2006; CPJ Venezuela 2006; RCTV closure announcement

2007.assessment:
Very serious, deteriorating sharply. RCTV closed May 2007—most-watched Venezuelan
channel; replaced by state channel TVES. International condemnation. Thousands
protested RCTV closure. RSF condemned closure as major blow to Venezuelan press
freedom. Globovision remained independent but under constant regulatory threat.
Self-censorship in remaining private media dramatically increased after RCTV.
CPJ documented approximately 40+ journalist attacks and threats in 2007.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2007; CPJ Venezuela 2007; RCTV closure documentation

2008.assessment:
Very serious. Post-RCTV closure chilling effect on remaining media. State media
(VTV, VIVE, TVES) dominant. Globovision last major independent TV broadcaster.
Cadena (mandatory government broadcast interruptions) used hundreds of times
per year. Internet beginning to be used by independent journalists but limited
connectivity. Criminal defamation laws (RESORTE amendments) threatening online
content. Regional independent press under financial pressure.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2008; CPJ Venezuela 2008

2009.assessment:
Very serious. RESORTE amendments (2009) extended broadcast restrictions to
internet content. Conatel ordered dozens of radio stations closed (May 2009:
32 stations; August 2009: 240+ stations closed or threatened). Newspaper
Globovision fined. Internet freedom restrictions beginning. CPJ documented
continued journalist harassment and prosecution. Private newspapers still
publishing but under financial pressure from government advertising withdrawal.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2009; CPJ Venezuela 2009; Conatel station closures

2010.assessment:
Very serious. Additional broadcast law restrictions. Globovision fined repeatedly
by Conatel. Foreign correspondents facing harassment. Social media users
prosecuted under RESORTE. Internet restrictions advancing. Newspaper imports
constrained by currency controls reducing newsprint access. CPJ documented
Venezuela among worst press freedom situations in Americas. IPI and RSF
condemned systematic media capture.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2010; CPJ Venezuela 2010

2011.assessment:
Very serious. Globovision still broadcasting critically but under severe financial
pressure. Criminal prosecutions of journalists continuing. Internet content
restrictions advancing. Newsprint access constrained (currency controls limiting
paper imports; CADIVI restricting newsprint dollars). Self-censorship near-universal
in broadcast media. Chavez cancer announced 2011 generating further restrictions
on reporting health information.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2011; CPJ Venezuela 2011

2012.assessment:
Very serious. Re-election campaign (October 2012) with severe media imbalance:
state media and captured private media heavily pro-Chavez; Globovision only
independent TV. Cadena interruptions used extensively during campaign. Opposition
candidate Capriles received minimal state media coverage. Print media critical
but circulation declining from newsprint restrictions.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2012; CPJ Venezuela 2012

2013.assessment:
Very serious. Globovision sold to pro-government investor (2013); ended critical
TV broadcasting. Venezuela now had no independent national TV broadcaster.
All national TV either state or captured. Print press still partially independent
(El Nacional, El Universal, Tal Cual) but under severe advertising and newsprint
pressure. Maduro narrowly won April election; media used extensively for propaganda.
CPJ named Venezuela one of worst for journalist imprisonment in Americas.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2013; CPJ Venezuela 2013; Globovision sale

2014.assessment:
Very serious, approaching no press freedom. La Salida protests (February-May 2014)
with severe media repression: journalists beaten, cameras confiscated, staff
arrested covering protests. CPJ documented multiple journalists arrested and
physically attacked by security forces and colectivos. Social media increasingly
blocked during protests. Internet speeds throttled. Foreign journalists expelled.
CNN, NTN24 ordered off air during protest coverage. El Universal and El Nacional
sold to pro-government investors (2014-2015 process).

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2014; CPJ Venezuela 2014; La Salida media repression

2015.assessment:
Very serious. RSF ranked Venezuela 137th of 180 countries. El Universal and
El Nacional sold to pro-government owners; effectively silenced critical print
press. Diosdado Cabello filed defamation lawsuits against foreign media
(WSJ, ABC, NTN24) and Venezuelan journalists. Opposition won December 2015
Assembly supermajority; government responded with increased media repression.
Newsprint shortages critical; several regional newspapers suspended publication.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2015; CPJ Venezuela 2015

2016.assessment:
No press freedom emerging. RSF ranked Venezuela among worst in Americas.
Most print newspapers suspended or online-only due to newsprint crisis (CADIVI
blocking newsprint imports). Tal Cual moved online-only. Economic crisis destroying
advertising market; independent outlets unable to survive financially.
Internet increasingly the only independent news medium. Government blocking
websites critical of Maduro. Self-censorship total in broadcast sector.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2016; CPJ Venezuela 2016

2017.assessment:
No press freedom. Constituent assembly crisis (July 2017) with extreme media
repression: journalist arrests during protests, equipment confiscated, Venezuelan
media employees arrested or detained. ANC (constituent assembly) claiming
power to censor media. CPJ documented dozens of cases. IPYS Venezuela (press
freedom organization) reported 500+ press freedom violations in 2017.
International journalists and crews attacked or expelled. RSF ranked Venezuela
among 10 worst globally.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2017; CPJ Venezuela 2017; IPYS Venezuela 2017

2018.assessment:
No press freedom. Maduro fraudulent re-election (May 2018) with zero independent
media coverage. State media completely dominant. Digital news outlets harassed;
VPItv blocked; La Patilla, El Nacional online under attack. Internet blocks
common during protests. FAES killings (5,287) covered only in international
media. IPYS Venezuela documented 900+ press freedom violations. CPJ named
Venezuela among world's worst for press freedom alongside Syria, Turkey, China.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2018; CPJ Venezuela 2018; IPYS Venezuela 2018

2019.assessment:
No press freedom. Dual government crisis (Guaido vs Maduro) with extreme
repression of coverage. CNN en Espanol, BBC, NTN24, Deutsche Welle blocked.
Internet shutdowns during key political events (January-May 2019). Foreign
journalists physically expelled or denied visas. VPItv staff arrested.
CONATEL instructed broadcasters to carry only official government feed during
coup attempt (April 30). RSF ranked Venezuela 148th globally. IPYS documented
1,000+ press freedom violations.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2019; CPJ Venezuela 2019; IPYS Venezuela 2019

2020.assessment:
No press freedom. COVID pandemic used to further restrict independent reporting.
Presidential decree criminalized news spreading alarm (April 2020)—used
against journalists reporting COVID casualties. Journalists arrested for
COVID coverage. Internet speeds further throttled. ANC decree restricting
media content. Remaining independent digital outlets (Efecto Cocuyo, Tal Cual,
El Pitazo) operating under constant threat. Staff self-censoring or operating
from exile. GDP trough; media economically unviable.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2020; CPJ Venezuela 2020; IPYS Venezuela 2020

2021.assessment:
No press freedom. Dollarization generating limited economic stabilization but
no improvement in press freedom. Anti-Hate Law (2017) used against journalists.
Internet blocking (CANTV—state telecom) frequent for VPN services and
critical sites. Foreign correspondents operating with significant risk.
Maduro government reaching superficial diplomatic agreements (Barbados context
beginning) but no press freedom improvements. IPYS documented 800+ violations.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2021; CPJ Venezuela 2021; IPYS Venezuela 2021

2022.assessment:
No press freedom. Consistent severe repression. CPJ named Venezuela among
worst 10 globally. Journalists self-censoring or exiled. Digital news outlets
(Efecto Cocuyo, El Pitazo, Runrun.es) operating but under constant harassment.
IPYS documented 800+ violations including arrests, equipment confiscation,
website blocking. RSF ranking ~159/180. No improvement in structural conditions.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2022; CPJ Venezuela 2022; IPYS Venezuela 2022

2023.assessment:
No press freedom. Barbados Accord (October 2023) generated brief optimism
but included no press freedom provisions. CPJ documented Venezuela's systemic
media repression as worst in Americas. Internet blocking continuing. CONATEL
harassment. Pre-2024 election period: opposition journalists targeted for
covering Guaido dissolution and primary elections. RSF ranking remained
among worst 20-25 globally.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2023; CPJ Venezuela 2023; IPYS Venezuela 2023

2024.assessment:
No press freedom, re-intensifying. July 2024 election: journalists covering
opposition results documentation targeted; equipment confiscated; staff
detained. Post-election crackdown: hundreds arrested including journalists.
Social media and messaging apps blocked during critical post-election period.
Gonzalez Urrutia (opposition candidate) forced into exile. Foreign journalists
expelled or denied access. IPYS documented record violations in election year.

sources.citation: RSF Press Freedom Index 2024; CPJ Venezuela 2024; IPYS Venezuela 2024

2025.assessment:
No press freedom projected to continue. Post-election consolidation of media
repression. No independent national TV broadcaster. Print press collapsed.
Independent digital media operating from exile or under constant threat.
CANTV internet blocking routine. Anti-Hate Law and RESORTE used against
any critical content. Venezuela among 5-10 worst globally for press freedom.
No structural improvement expected under Maduro government.

sources.citation: RSF 2025 projections; CPJ Venezuela 2025

1995.assessment:
Moderate statistical transparency. OCEI (Central Statistical Office) functioning
under Caldera government. National census conducted 1990; next scheduled 2001.
BCV (Central Bank) publishing regular monetary and economic data. GDP, inflation,
trade statistics produced with reasonable timeliness. Oil sector statistics
reliable (PDVSA publishing). World Bank SCI series begins 2004; estimated
~55 for 1995 based on functioning statistical institutions.

sources.citation: OCEI Venezuela institutional history; World Bank Venezuela statistical assessment

1996.assessment:
Moderate statistical transparency. BCV publishing regular reports. Inflation
data monthly. GDP quarterly. Balance of payments published. Oil sector data
from PDVSA. Social statistics (poverty, employment) from OCEI surveys.
Currency crisis data accurately reported (hyperinflation context in 1996).

sources.citation: Estimate; BCV publishing history

1997.assessment:
Moderate-good statistical transparency. PDVSA financial statements published
(Apertura period required disclosure to international investors). BCV credible.
OCEI household survey (ENCOVI) producing poverty estimates. IMF Article IV
consultations completed with published staff reports. Venezuela considered
a relatively well-documented economy by regional standards.

sources.citation: Estimate; PDVSA financial disclosure period

1998.assessment:
Moderate statistical transparency. Pre-Chavez era; statistical institutions
functional. BCV independent and credible. Economic crisis data (oil crash)
accurately reported. Some electoral year distortions in reporting positive
indicators. OCEI continuing household surveys. IMF consultation data published.

sources.citation: Estimate; BCV pre-Chavez independence

1999.assessment:
Moderate but declining. Constituent assembly disrupting government institutions
including statistics offices. OCEI renamed INE (Instituto Nacional de Estadistica,
2000 law). Transition period causing publication delays. BCV technically
independent but Chavez government pressuring on data presentation. GDP contraction
(-6%) data published. Some uncertainty about data integrity beginning.

sources.citation: Estimate; OCEI-INE transition period

2000.assessment:
Moderate. INE (new statistics institute) continuing household surveys and
census work. BCV publishing monetary data. GDP, inflation, trade data available.
Misiones social spending tracked partially by INE. Social statistics (poverty)
becoming politically sensitive; government pressure on INE to show improvement
in social indicators. National census scheduled for 2001.

sources.citation: Estimate; INE established; social statistics politicization beginning

2001.assessment:
Moderate. Census 2001 conducted (published 2001-2002)—major achievement.
BCV publishing. Oil sector statistics maintained. INE publishing poverty and
employment surveys. Enabling law (November 2001) concentrating statistical
authority in executive—INE mandated to show positive social trends.
PDVSA transparency beginning to decline as government control increased.

sources.citation: Estimate; Census 2001 conducted

2002.assessment:
Moderate but declining. Coup (April 2002) and oil strike (December 2002-February
2003) disrupting economic data collection. PDVSA financial statements becoming
less transparent. BCV publishing but political pressure increasing. GDP crisis
data (-8.9% in 2002) published—honest about crisis depth. INE continuing
surveys despite disruption.

sources.citation: Estimate; coup/strike disruption

2003.assessment:
Moderate but declining. Post-strike PDVSA financial statements classified;
no public disclosure of oil revenues. INE continuing surveys but misiones
spending not captured in national accounts. CADIVI (currency controls)
creating multiple exchange rates; official figures increasingly diverging
from economic reality. GDP -7.8% data published honestly.

sources.citation: Estimate; PDVSA classified; currency controls

2004.assessment:
First WB SCI measurement: 53/100 — moderate. Recall referendum win (August
2004); political stability enabling some statistical improvement. INE publishing
regularly. BCV credible on monetary data. Oil boom revenues visible in GDP
but off-budget spending via FONDEN not in national accounts. GDP growth (+18.3%)
accurately reported. Poverty data (INE) showing improvement from social missions.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2004

2005.assessment:
Moderate (55). Improving SCI score reflects INE survey activities and census-based
estimates. FONDEN off-budget spending not in national accounts—significant gap.
BCV monetary data published. PDVSA financial statements unpublished; oil revenue
data inferred from international oil price data. INE poverty surveys showing
improvement. Employment statistics published regularly.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2005

2006.assessment:
Moderate (57). SCI peak period for Venezuela. INE household surveys and
economic statistics generally published. BCV independent and publishing.
Oil sector data from external sources supplementing PDVSA opacity. Inflation
accurately reported (13.7% 2006). GDP growth data published. Social indicators
showing genuine improvement from oil-funded missions.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2006

2007.assessment:
Moderate, beginning to decline. CANTV nationalization (2007) bringing telecom
data collection under state control. Nationalizations complicating national
accounts. Inflation (18.7%) accurately reported. Exchange rate controls
(CADIVI) creating parallel market distortions. INE still publishing household
surveys. BCV maintaining credible publications but under executive pressure.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2007

2008.assessment:
Moderate. BCV publishing inflation, GDP, monetary data. Inflation (31.4%)
accurately reported despite political inconvenience. INE household surveys
continuing. Nationalization wave (cement, steel, food) complicating national
accounts methodology. PDVSA production and revenue data unavailable. Exchange
rate distortions growing with parallel market.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2008

2009.assessment:
Moderate, declining. GDP recession (-3.2%) published. Additional nationalizations
complicating national accounts. Inflation (28.6%) published. BCV beginning
to come under pressure to present favorable data. IMF Article IV consultations
becoming less cooperative. PDVSA data entirely unavailable. INE publications
continuing but with quality concerns.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2009

2010.assessment:
Moderate but declining. GDP (-1.5%) published. Inflation (28.2%) published.
However increasing delays in publication. Poverty data from INE beginning to
show politically motivated positive spin. CADIVI multiple exchange rates making
GDP comparisons unreliable. IMF noted Venezuela's declining statistical quality
in Article IV consultations.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2010

2011.assessment:
Moderate. GDP growth (4.2%) published. BCV publishing monetary aggregates and
inflation (26.1%). INE continuing surveys. Chavez cancer (announced June 2011)
not affecting statistical publications. However PDVSA financial data entirely
opaque; China-Venezuela loans off-balance sheet; FONDEN spending not in
national accounts. Quality concerns mounting.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2011

2012.assessment:
Moderate, declining. GDP growth (5.6%) published. Inflation (21.1%) published
but declining accuracy suspected. BCV credibility weakening under political
pressure. INE 2011 census published. Multi-tier exchange rates (CADIVI/SICAD)
making national accounts methodology opaque. IMF consultations constrained.
WB SCI declining trend established.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2012

2013.assessment:
Poor and deteriorating sharply. BCV stopped publishing GDP quarterly data
on schedule. Annual inflation figures published but widely doubted (inflation
reported 56.2% while independent estimates higher). Chavez death (March 2013)
and political transition. SCAD multi-tier exchange system making real economic
data essentially unknowable. IMF Article IV consultations stalled. WB SCI
declining sharply. Venezuela's statistical system entering dysfunction.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2013

2014.assessment:
Poor. BCV ceased regular publication of key economic indicators. GDP entered
recession (-3.9%) but published late and with doubted methodology. Inflation
officially 68.5% but independent estimates (e.g., Johns Hopkins Prof. Steve
Hanke) much higher. National accounts methodology opaque. IMF noting Venezuela
as special data dissemination problems case. INE poverty surveys suspended.
WB SCI declining toward 40.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2014; IMF Venezuela Article IV

2015.assessment:
Very poor. BCV last published official inflation data for 2015 (officially
181.5% but independent estimates 200-300%). After 2015 BCV stopped publishing
virtually all data. GDP published late and inaccurate. INE poverty surveys
suspended 2015. WB SCI 33/100. IMF unable to complete standard Article IV
consultation. Venezuela designated SDDS-non-compliant (Special Data Dissemination
Standard). Statistical system effectively collapsed.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2015; IMF Venezuela

2016.assessment:
Very poor. BCV ceased publishing nearly all economic statistics. No official
inflation data published. No GDP data. No monetary aggregates. National Assembly
(opposition) published some economic data that government disputed. IMF using
partner-country data and models to estimate Venezuela's statistics. WB SCI 25/100.
Only employment data from INE occasionally published. Venezuela effectively
a statistical black hole.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2016

2017.assessment:
Near-total statistical collapse. WB SCI 17/100. No official economic data
published by BCV or INE for full year. Hyperinflation (2,616%) estimated
by IMF and independent economists only—not published by Venezuela. GDP
-15% estimated by IMF only. PDVSA default (November 2017). IMF unable to
conduct Article IV. National Assembly published estimates government disputed.
Venezuela at bottom of Western Hemisphere statistical capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2017

2018.assessment:
Total statistical collapse. WB SCI ~15/100. No official economic data whatsoever.
Hyperinflation 65,374%—all IMF estimates. GDP -19%—all IMF estimates. No
official poverty, employment, health, or economic statistics. BCV completely
ceased publishing. PDVSA production decline (1.2M bbl/day) measured by OPEC
third-party data, not Venezuela. OHCHR documenting FAES killings (5,287) that
government denied. Complete absence of state statistical function.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2018

2019.assessment:
Total statistical collapse. WB SCI ~14/100. Dual government (Maduro vs Guaido)
each claiming statistical authority; neither publishing credible data. Hyperinflation
344,510%—all external estimates. GDP -27%—all external estimates. No health,
education, poverty, or employment statistics. UNHCR refugee data (4.5M+) produced
externally. US PDVSA sanctions making oil production tracking via OPEC/third-party
only source. Venezuela among 3-4 worst globally for statistical capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2019

2020.assessment:
Total statistical collapse continuing. COVID pandemic made health statistics
completely inaccessible—Venezuela unable/unwilling to report COVID deaths.
Pan American Health Organization (PAHO) used indirect methods to estimate
excess mortality. GDP trough (~$1,548/capita)—all external estimates. BCV
published limited exchange rate data under dollarization but no macroeconomic
data. WB SCI ~14/100.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2020

2021.assessment:
Very poor but marginal improvement. BCV resumed publishing limited monetary
data (exchange rates, some aggregates) as dollarization brought partial
normalization. GDP estimates beginning to be produced (2021 ~+3%). IMF
resumed more active engagement with Venezuela data. Inflation declining (686%)
but still external estimates. INE did not publish poverty surveys. WB SCI
slightly improved reflecting BCV partial resumption.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Performance Index 2021

2022.assessment:
Very poor but slowly improving from trough. BCV publishing exchange rate
and limited monetary data. GDP growth estimates published (~5% 2022). Some
inflation data (172%). However no household surveys, no poverty data, no
employment statistics, no comprehensive national accounts. Oil production
data from OPEC/PDVSA OPEC reports only. Fundamental statistical incapacity
unchanged; marginal recovery from 2019-2020 trough.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Performance Index 2022

2023.assessment:
Very poor, minimal improvement. Barbados Accord context generating some
statistical cooperation. BCV publishing broader monetary data. GDP estimates
published (~3-5%). Some sectoral data becoming available. However no poverty
surveys, no employment statistics, no comprehensive national accounts, no
PDVSA financial data. IMF engagement improved but still based largely on
external estimates. At/near the floor of the very_poor category.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Performance Index 2023 estimate

2024.assessment:
Very poor. Post-election (July 2024) crackdown likely reversed marginal
improvements. Sanctions reimposed. International statistical engagement
constrained. BCV publishing exchange rate data but macroeconomic data minimal.
No household surveys. Election-year political sensitivity further suppressing
any statistical transparency. GDP growth data dependent on external estimation.

sources.citation: WB SPI 2024 estimate; analyst consensus

2025.assessment:
Very poor projected. No structural improvement in statistical capacity.
Venezuela remains among bottom 5-10% globally for statistical transparency.
BCV publishing limited exchange rate and aggregate monetary data. No comprehensive
national accounts, no household surveys, no poverty/employment data, no PDVSA
financial disclosures. IMF reliant on external estimation for all key indicators.
Fundamental statistical dysfunction continues under authoritarian Maduro regime.

sources.citation: WB SPI 2025 projection; IMF Venezuela data assessment
